target.title: fix(models): preserve source snapshots for SecretRef providers
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw models status --probe` could resolve `models.providers.*.apiKey` SecretRefs, then drop the pre-resolution source snapshot before downstream `models.json` regeneration.
- Solution: always keep the source config snapshot paired with the resolved runtime snapshot so `models.json` generation can persist non-secret markers instead of resolved provider secrets.
- What changed: `loadModelsConfigWithSource()` now preserves `sourceConfig` in the runtime snapshot for both pinned and unpinned paths, and regression coverage now locks both the snapshot handoff and a custom-provider `models.json` write.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not alter SecretRef marker formats, `secrets audit` classification rules, or provider-specific auth marker policy.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #84632
- Related #84376
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

- `models status --probe` is a normal operator workflow, and the regression could rewrite agent `models.json` with resolved provider credentials for custom providers.
- That breaks the documented SecretRef persistence contract and creates a real plaintext-on-disk secret exposure.
- The affected path is already used by adjacent commands (`models list`, `models status`, `models scan`, alias/auth helpers), so the fix needs to re-anchor the runtime/source snapshot boundary instead of papering over one provider.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `models status`/`models.json` regeneration now preserves the pre-resolution source snapshot so SecretRef-backed custom provider `apiKey` values persist as non-secret markers instead of resolved plaintext secrets.
- Real environment tested: local source checkout on macOS with a temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, and `OPENCLAW_AGENT_DIR`; no gateway token configured, so command-secret resolution fell back to the same local SecretRef path the issue report called out.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `env -i PATH="$PATH" HOME="$TMP_HOME" TMPDIR="$TMP_TMPDIR" OPENCLAW_MODEL_LITELLM_API_KEY='sk-live-proof-litellm-123456789' node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'NODE' ... loadModelsConfigWithSource({ commandName: 'models status' }); await ensureOpenClawModelsJson(loaded.resolvedConfig, agentDir); ... NODE`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): terminal output after the patched flow:
```json
{
  "diagnostics": [
    "models status: gateway secrets.resolve unavailable (gateway closed (1008): unauthorized: gateway token missing (set gateway.remote.token to match gateway.auth.token)\nGateway target: ws://127.0.0.1:18789\nSource: local loopback\nConfig: /tmp/.../openclaw.json\nBind: loopback); resolved command secrets locally."
  ],
  "resolvedApiKey": "sk-live-proof-litellm-123456789",
  "persistedApiKey": "OPENCLAW_MODEL_LITELLM_API_KEY",
  "persistedContainsResolvedSecret": false,
  "modelsPath": "/tmp/.../agent/models.json"
}
```
- Observed result after fix: the resolved runtime config still sees the live provider secret, but the regenerated `agent/models.json` stores only `OPENCLAW_MODEL_LITELLM_API_KEY`, and the written file does not contain the resolved secret bytes.
- What was not tested: I did not run a full live gateway `openclaw models status --probe` against a remote authenticated gateway or a multi-agent heartbeat regeneration lane in this local worktree.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before this patch, the unpinned branch in `src/commands/models/load-config.ts` called `setRuntimeConfigSnapshot(resolvedConfig)` without `sourceConfig`, which left downstream `ensureOpenClawModelsJson()` with only the resolved runtime values.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `loadModelsConfigWithSource()` correctly derived `sourceConfig = pinnedSourceConfig ?? runtimeConfig`, but in the unpinned branch it stored only `resolvedConfig` in the runtime snapshot. That erased the pre-resolution source config before `models.json` generation reused the snapshot, so the persistence path could no longer recover SecretRef-managed markers for custom-provider `apiKey` values.
- Missing detection / guardrail: we had coverage for source-snapshot projection and marker persistence, but not for the specific unpinned `loadModelsConfigWithSource()` path that resolves command secrets and then regenerates custom-provider `models.json` state.
- Contributing context (if known): provenance is clear from `5b063c2d83` (`fix: keep legacy config repair in doctor`), which introduced the pinned/unpinned branch split and left the unpinned branch without the companion source snapshot.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/commands/models/load-config.test.ts`, `src/agents/models-config.runtime-source-snapshot.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: command-secret resolution for `models status` must keep the source snapshot attached, and a custom provider with an env-backed SecretRef `apiKey` must regenerate `models.json` with the env marker instead of the resolved secret.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives at the handoff between command secret resolution and `models.json` generation; these tests cover exactly that boundary without dragging in unrelated provider runtime or gateway behavior.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): the pre-existing runtime source snapshot suite already covered projected clones and header markers, but not the unpinned command-load path for custom-provider api keys.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `openclaw models status` and any downstream `models.json` regeneration path that uses `loadModelsConfigWithSource()` now preserve SecretRef-backed custom provider `apiKey` markers instead of rewriting resolved plaintext values.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
models status -> resolve SecretRef apiKey -> runtime snapshot keeps only resolved config -> models.json regeneration writes resolved secret

After:
models status -> resolve SecretRef apiKey -> runtime snapshot keeps resolved config + source snapshot -> models.json regeneration writes source marker
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: this change narrows secret persistence behavior by preserving the source snapshot needed to write non-secret markers. It does not expand secret access; it prevents resolved provider secrets from being written into agent `models.json`.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local source checkout, Node 24, temporary config/state/agent dirs
- Model/provider: custom `litellm` provider with env-backed SecretRef `apiKey`
- Integration/channel (if any): none
- Relevant config (redacted): `models.providers.litellm.apiKey = { source: "env", provider: "default", id: "OPENCLAW_MODEL_LITELLM_API_KEY" }`

### Steps

1. Write a temporary `openclaw.json` with a custom provider whose `apiKey` is an env-backed SecretRef.
2. Export `OPENCLAW_MODEL_LITELLM_API_KEY` with a redacted test secret and call `loadModelsConfigWithSource({ commandName: "models status" })`.
3. Call `ensureOpenClawModelsJson(loaded.resolvedConfig, agentDir)` and inspect the generated `agent/models.json`.

### Expected

- The resolved runtime config may hold the live secret for command execution.
- The generated `agent/models.json` must persist only a non-secret marker derived from the source config.

### Actual

- After the patch, `resolvedApiKey` was the live test secret, `persistedApiKey` was `OPENCLAW_MODEL_LITELLM_API_KEY`, and the generated file did not contain the resolved secret bytes.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [x] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: focused Vitest regression coverage for `loadModelsConfigWithSource()` and runtime source snapshot persistence; live local module execution proving regenerated `models.json` writes the env marker for a custom provider instead of the resolved secret.
- Edge cases checked: unpinned source-snapshot path; custom provider env SecretRef rather than only bundled/core provider markers.
- What you did **not** verify: full remote gateway probe, multi-agent heartbeat-triggered regeneration, and broader `pnpm check:changed`/Testbox lanes from this Codex worktree.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: other commands that intentionally relied on `loadModelsConfigWithSource()` clearing the source snapshot could now observe the paired source config.
  - Mitigation: the source snapshot is already the documented companion for runtime-derived config writes, and focused tests now lock the intended command-loader behavior.
- Risk: this fix could mask a separate marker-format bug by making the source snapshot available again.
  - Mitigation: this PR stays scoped to the root regression path and leaves marker-format policy untouched; issue #84376 remains the right place for codex sentinel classification specifics.

target.title: telegram: allow last blockquote to be expandable
target.body:
## Summary - Adds Telegram formatter support for Telegram's native expandable blockquote HTML form. - Preserves existing normal blockquote rendering. - Adds formatter coverage for expandable blockquotes. ## Changes - Render expandable blockquote metadata as `
`. - Preserve existing blockquote rendering behavior. - Add formatter coverage for expandable blockquotes. - Problem: OpenClaw's Telegram formatter could not represent Telegram's native expandable blockquote HTML form. - Solution: Add explicit formatter support for expandable blockquotes. - What changed: Telegram blockquote rendering and related formatter tests. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Gateway orchestration, runtime delivery, auth, model routing, and default channel behavior. ## Motivation Telegram supports expandable blockquotes natively. OpenClaw's Telegram formatter should be able to emit that supported HTML form when markdown/IR metadata requests it. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related #84082 - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Coordination with #84082 Related PR #84082 handles the raw Telegram HTML sanitizer path for user-supplied `
` markup. This PR handles the generated formatter path: markdown/formatter input can opt in with `expandFinalBlockquote: true` and emit Telegram-native `
` output. These paths are complementary and can land separately. This PR does not depend on #84082 because it generates trusted Telegram HTML after markdown formatting rather than preserving a user-supplied raw HTML attribute through sanitizer cleanup. If maintainers want both raw-HTML passthrough and generated formatter support, #84082 and this PR should remain aligned on the same Telegram-native output form. ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram expandable blockquote formatting was verified in a real OpenClaw setup using the Telegram integration. - Real environment tested: OpenClaw 2026.5.18 installed runtime on macOS with Telegram channel enabled. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the installed-runtime formatter command below, then observed the rendered message in Telegram in collapsed and expanded states. - Evidence after fix: Live formatter command output plus Telegram screenshots: - Collapsed screenshot: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b590a21f-5b43-4bea-b917-9bba48fa42e2 - Expanded screenshot: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/77508c27-954a-40bb-a0ae-e4666a08d95e - Console output: ```html
Usage: 1 in / 1 out · cache 0/0 · est $0.01 GPT-5.5: expandable signature verification Grok: none Ollama-14B: none session agent:main:telegram:direct:7456384535
``` - Observed result after fix: Telegram displayed the trailing blockquote collapsed initially and expanded it in-place when tapped. The formatter emitted Telegram HTML containing `
` when `expandFinalBlockquote: true` was passed. - What was not tested: Other Telegram clients beyond the tested client. Formatter command: ```sh node --input-type=module <<'EOF' const mod = await import('file:///opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/format-AZK6G0is.js'); const sample = [ 'DM expandable signature test', '', '> Usage: 1 in / 1 out · cache 0/0 · est $0.01', '> GPT-5.5: expandable signature verification', '> Grok: none', '> Ollama-14B: none', '> session agent:main:telegram:direct:7456384535' ].join('\n'); console.log(mod.a(sample, { textMode: 'markdown', expandFinalBlockquote: true })); EOF ``` Formatter output: ```html
Usage: 1 in / 1 out · cache 0/0 · est $0.01 GPT-5.5: expandable signature verification Grok: none Ollama-14B: none session agent:main:telegram:direct:7456384535
``` ## Verification - Ran the Telegram formatter tests. - Verified live Telegram rendering in a real OpenClaw installation. - Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram formatter can now emit native expandable blockquote HTML. - Real environment tested: OpenClaw 2026.5.18 installed runtime on macOS with Telegram channel enabled. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the live installed-runtime formatter check above, then verified the rendered result in Telegram with collapsed and expanded screenshots. - Evidence after fix: See the Evidence section below for the live formatter command and Telegram screenshots. - Observed result after fix: Telegram displays the blockquote collapsed initially and expands it when tapped. - What was not tested: Other Telegram clients beyond the tested client. - Before evidence: Formatter lacked an expandable blockquote output path. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Telegram formatter only emitted normal blockquote HTML and did not carry expandable blockquote metadata through to Telegram's native `

- move post-run auth-profile success bookkeeping off the reply-blocking embedded-run path

target.title: fix: render Codex source replies in TUI
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes TUI rendering for Codex source replies that are delivered through the `message` tool instead of the normal assistant-text final path.

This PR:
- preserves source-reply metadata through reply normalization, media handling, and TTS paths
- broadcasts message-tool-only source replies onto the live chat stream so `openclaw tui` renders them immediately
- keeps long Codex source-reply turns alive after reasoning completes while waiting for delayed visible `message` calls
- keeps the TUI input blocked while a backend run is still active after the watchdog notice, instead of encouraging duplicate submissions
- avoids duplicate/empty terminal chat events from replayed source-reply finals

## Verification

Behavior addressed: Codex replies could be visible through some surfaces/history refreshes but not render immediately in `openclaw tui`.

Real environment tested: OCM local runtimes using `CodexClaw-tui-latest` and `CodexClaw-tui-beta`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```sh

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-media-paths.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts -- --runInBand
ocm runtime build-local tui-source-reply-fix --repo . --force
ocm start CodexClaw-tui-latest --runtime tui-source-reply-fix

```

Evidence after fix:
- Focused local Vitest pass: 6 files, 588 tests passed.

- User verified the local TUI behavior against the rebuilt runtime.

- Testbox id: `tbx_01ksaspa0jp9ytxkzxd431fbdh`.
- Actions run: `26337467682`.

Observed result after fix:
- `openclaw tui` receives live source-reply finals instead of waiting for a later history refresh.
- Long Codex reasoning-then-message responses remain active long enough for the visible reply to arrive.
- TUI no longer reopens the prompt while the backend turn is still running.

target.title: fix(install): align install-cli.sh Node default to 24.15.0
target.body:
## Problem

`install.sh` defaults to Node 24 (`NODE_DEFAULT_MAJOR=24`) and docs recommend Node 24, but `install-cli.sh` defaults to Node 22.22.0 (`OPENCLAW_NODE_VERSION:-22.22.0`). This mismatch means:

- Users following the main installer get Node 24
- Users using local-prefix install get Node 22
- Native modules rebuilt with the shell Node (24) produce ABI errors when loaded by the managed Node (22):
  ```
  NODE_MODULE_VERSION 137
  This version of Node.js requires NODE_MODULE_VERSION 127
  ```

## Fix

Update `install-cli.sh` default from `22.22.0` to `24.15.0` (current Node 24 LTS), matching `install.sh` and the docs recommendation. Users can still pin a specific version via `--node-version` or `OPENCLAW_NODE_VERSION`.

Fixes #79558

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: install-cli.sh now defaults to Node 24.15.0, matching install.sh and eliminating the Node 22 vs 24 mismatch that caused native module ABI errors.
Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js v22.16.0, patched source checkout at `/tmp/fix-82932` (commit ac1399).

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
Step 1 -- verify the default value in the patched install-cli.sh. Step 2 -- run the installer help to show the new default. Step 3 -- cross-reference with install.sh to confirm alignment.

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

**Patched install-cli.sh default:**
```console
$ grep "NODE_VERSION=" scripts/install-cli.sh | head -1
NODE_VERSION="${OPENCLAW_NODE_VERSION:-24.15.0}"
```

**Help output confirms new default:**
```console
$ bash scripts/install-cli.sh --help 2>&1 | grep "node-version"
  --node-version <ver>                Node version (default: 24.15.0)
```

**Cross-reference with install.sh (already uses Node 24):**
```console
$ grep NODE_DEFAULT_MAJOR scripts/install.sh | head -1
NODE_DEFAULT_MAJOR=24
```

**Docs match:**
```console
$ grep "node-version" docs/install/installer.md
| `--node-version <ver>`      | Node version (default: `24.15.0`)                                               |
```

**Syntax validation:**
```console
$ bash -n scripts/install-cli.sh && echo "syntax valid"
syntax valid
```

Observed result after fix: Both installer paths (`install.sh` and `install-cli.sh`) now default to Node 24. The help text, source default, and documentation all show `24.15.0`. Before the fix, `install-cli.sh` defaulted to `22.22.0` while `install.sh` used `NODE_DEFAULT_MAJOR=24`, causing version mismatch and potential native module ABI errors.

What was not tested: Actual local-prefix install with the new default (would require removing the existing Node installation). The fix is a default value change only; the Node download and installation logic is unchanged.

target.title: fix(install): add pipe guard to prevent stdin stealing in curl | bash
target.body:
## Problem

When invoked via `curl -fsSL https://openclaw.ai/install.sh | bash`, bash reads the script incrementally from stdin. Interactive prompts (gum, read) and child processes that also read stdin steal bytes from the script stream, causing:

1. Truncated function names: `warn_shell_path_missing_di` instead of `warn_shell_path_missing_dir`
2. "command not found" errors for the truncated names
3. Indefinite hangs (the script stream is corrupted, bash blocks waiting for more input)

Reported on WSL2 Ubuntu 24.04 with 3/3 reproduction rate.

## Fix

Add a pipe guard at the top of `install.sh` that detects piped execution via empty `BASH_SOURCE[0]` (piped scripts have no source file), buffers the remaining stdin to a temp file using `cat`, and re-executes from the file via `exec`. This frees stdin for interactive prompts and child processes.

The guard:
- **Does not fire** for direct execution (`bash install.sh`) or process substitution (`bash <(curl ...)`) because `BASH_SOURCE` is set
- **Does not fire** for CI/Docker with non-tty stdin when running from a file
- **Cleans up** the temp file immediately after re-execution starts
- Uses `_OPENCLAW_PIPE_BUFFERED` env var to prevent infinite re-execution loops

Fixes #73814

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: the installer now buffers itself to a temp file when piped via curl, preventing child processes from consuming stdin bytes that corrupt the script stream.
Real environment tested: Linux x86_64, OpenClaw 2026.5.12 installed, Node v26.0.0, bash 5.2. Tested both piped execution (`cat install.sh | bash -s -- --install-method npm`) and direct execution (`bash install.sh --install-method npm < /dev/null`).
Exact steps or command run after this patch: (1) verified pipe guard pattern with a minimal test script that includes a deliberate stdin steal (`head -c 50 < /dev/stdin`); (2) ran full installer in piped mode; (3) ran full installer in direct mode with non-tty stdin; (4) confirmed both complete successfully without truncation.
Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

Pipe guard pattern verification (minimal test script with deliberate stdin steal):
```
$ cat /tmp/pipe-test-steal.sh | bash
Before stdin steal
After stdin steal
Function executed correctly
Script completed
```
Without the guard, the `head -c 50` would consume 50 bytes of the script stream, truncating the function definition. With the guard, the script runs from a temp file so stdin stealing has no effect.

Full installer in piped mode (script runs from temp file):
```
$ cat scripts/install.sh | bash -s -- --install-method npm 2>&1 | tail -10
✓ OpenClaw installed

[3/3] Finalizing setup
· Running doctor to migrate settings
· Running doctor
✓ Doctor complete

🦞 OpenClaw installed successfully (2026.5.12)!
```
Note: error source shows `/tmp/tmp.uuj4JcpyzQ: line 2988` confirming the guard buffered to temp file.

Full installer in direct mode (BASH_SOURCE is set, guard does not fire):
```
$ bash scripts/install.sh --install-method npm < /dev/null 2>&1 | tail -10
✓ Doctor complete

🦞 OpenClaw installed successfully (2026.5.12)!
```
Note: error source shows `scripts/install.sh: line 3000` confirming guard did not fire.

Observed result after fix: piped execution completes successfully without truncated function names or hangs. Direct execution is unaffected. The pipe guard adds negligible overhead (one mktemp + cat + exec).
What was not tested: macOS bash 3.2 (BASH_SOURCE behavior may differ in very old bash versions). WSL2 Ubuntu 24.04 (the reporter's environment) was not tested directly, but the fix is bash-level and platform-independent.

target.title: fix(install): show progress during npm install in non-interactive mode
target.body:
## Problem

When running `curl -fsSL https://openclaw.ai/install.sh | bash`, gum is disabled because stdin is a pipe (`is_non_interactive_shell` returns true). Without gum, `run_npm_global_install` silently redirects all output to a temp log file (line 751):

```bash
"${cmd[@]}" >"$log" 2>&1
```

Users see **no feedback** during the npm install step, which can take 60+ seconds. The gum branch already shows "Installing OpenClaw package" via `run_with_spinner`, but the no-gum fallback is completely silent.

## Fix

Add `ui_info "Installing OpenClaw package"` before the silent npm command in the no-gum branch, matching the pattern from PR #71720 that was applied to `run_quiet_step`.

Closes #82305

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: in non-interactive mode (piped stdin, no gum), the npm install step now shows a progress message instead of running silently for 60+ seconds.
Real environment tested: Linux x86_64, OpenClaw 2026.5.12 already installed, Node v26.0.0, npm 11.12.1. Ran `bash scripts/install.sh --install-method npm < /dev/null` to simulate `curl | bash` non-interactive mode.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: (1) checked out upstream/main install.sh, ran `bash scripts/install.sh --install-method npm < /dev/null 2>&1` and captured output; (2) applied the one-line fix, ran the same command again and captured output; (3) compared the install section output.
Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

Before (upstream/main, no progress line):
```console
$ bash scripts/install.sh --install-method npm < /dev/null 2>&1 | grep -A2 "Installing OpenClaw v"
· Installing OpenClaw v2026.5.12
✓ OpenClaw npm package installed
✓ OpenClaw installed
```

After (with `ui_info` added):
```console
$ bash scripts/install.sh --install-method npm < /dev/null 2>&1 | grep -A3 "Installing OpenClaw v"
· Installing OpenClaw v2026.5.12
· Installing OpenClaw package
✓ OpenClaw npm package installed
✓ OpenClaw installed
```

Full install section before:
```
[2/3] Installing OpenClaw
✓ Git already installed
· Installing OpenClaw v2026.5.12
✓ OpenClaw npm package installed
✓ OpenClaw installed
```

Full install section after:
```
[2/3] Installing OpenClaw
✓ Git already installed
· Installing OpenClaw v2026.5.12
· Installing OpenClaw package
✓ OpenClaw npm package installed
✓ OpenClaw installed
```

Observed result after fix: the "Installing OpenClaw package" progress line now appears between the version announcement and the completion checkmark, matching the text shown by the gum spinner branch in interactive mode. No other output changed.
What was not tested: gum/spinner interactive path (gum is only available when stdin is a tty, which this non-interactive proof intentionally disables). The gum branch already shows this text via `run_with_spinner` and is unchanged by this PR.

target.title: fix(install): verify node binary after auto-install and suppress ioctl errors
target.body:
## Problem

The Node.js auto-installer reports "Node.js v22 installed" without verifying the binary is actually available on PATH. On WSL2 Ubuntu 24.04, the NodeSource setup script produces ioctl errors (interactive terminal prompts in a non-interactive context), apt may fail silently, but the installer prints a success message and then immediately crashes with "Node.js v22+ is required."

Two root causes:
1. The NodeSource setup script (`bash "$tmp"`) runs without `DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive`, so it attempts interactive terminal operations that produce ioctl errors on WSL2
2. The installer prints `ui_success "Node.js v24 installed"` unconditionally after the apt call, without verifying `node` is on PATH

## Fix

1. Pass `DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive` to the NodeSource setup script execution (both root and sudo paths)
2. Run `activate_supported_node_on_path` and verify the active node meets the minimum version requirement (`node_is_at_least_required`) before printing the success message. If node is missing or too old, report the actual failure and exit instead of falsely reporting success.

Fixes #73837
## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: The Node.js auto-installer now (1) passes `DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive` via `sudo -E env` to suppress ioctl errors on WSL2 while preserving the caller's environment (proxy, PATH, etc.), and (2) verifies the active node binary meets the minimum version after package installation before reporting success.

Real environment tested: macOS (Darwin 24.5.0), Node v26.0.0, bash 5.3.9, from patched source checkout at commit 24f4134a9b.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

Step 1. Verified patched script preserves both `-E` and `DEBIAN_FRONTEND`
```console
$ grep "sudo.*DEBIAN_FRONTEND" scripts/install.sh
        sudo env DEBIAN_FRONTEND="${DEBIAN_FRONTEND:-noninteractive}" NEEDRESTART_MODE="${NEEDRESTART_MODE:-a}" apt-get "$@"
                run_quiet_step "Configuring NodeSource repository" sudo -E env DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive bash "$tmp"
```
The `apt_get()` wrapper (line 1) uses `sudo env` with explicit vars. The NodeSource setup (line 2) uses `sudo -E env` to preserve the full caller environment while adding the noninteractive flag.

Step 2. Verified post-install guard rejects missing or too-old node
```console
$ grep -A3 "if ! node_is_at_least_required" scripts/install.sh
        if ! node_is_at_least_required; then
            local active_version
            active_version="$(node -v 2>/dev/null || echo "not found")"
            ui_error "Node.js v${NODE_MIN_VERSION}+ required but found ${active_version} after install"
```

Step 3. Exercised the guard functions from the patched `scripts/install.sh`
```console
$ bash -c '
source <(sed -n "/^NODE_MIN_MAJOR=/,/^activate_supported/p" scripts/install.sh | head -200)
source <(sed -n "/^parse_node_version_components_for_binary/,/^}/p" scripts/install.sh)
source <(sed -n "/^parse_node_version_components()/,/^}/p" scripts/install.sh)
source <(sed -n "/^node_is_at_least_required()/,/^}/p" scripts/install.sh)
NODE_MIN_MAJOR=22; NODE_MIN_MINOR=14; NODE_MIN_VERSION="22.14"

echo "=== Current node ==="
node_is_at_least_required && echo "PASS: $(node -v) >= v22.14"

echo "=== Missing node ==="
PATH="/nonexistent" node_is_at_least_required || echo "PASS: rejects missing node"

echo "=== Old node (v18) ==="
mkdir -p /tmp/fn; printf "#!/bin/bash\necho v18.0.0" > /tmp/fn/node; chmod +x /tmp/fn/node
PATH="/tmp/fn" node_is_at_least_required || echo "PASS: rejects v18.0.0"
rm -rf /tmp/fn
'
=== Current node ===
PASS: v26.0.0 >= v22.14
=== Missing node ===
PASS: rejects missing node
=== Old node (v18) ===
PASS: rejects v18.0.0
```

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the patched install.sh confirms:

```console
$ grep "sudo -E env DEBIAN_FRONTEND" scripts/install.sh
                run_quiet_step "Configuring NodeSource repository" sudo -E env DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive bash "$tmp"
```

The `-E` flag preserves the caller's environment (proxy, PATH, locale) while `env DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive` adds the noninteractive frontend flag. The post-install guard correctly rejects missing and too-old node binaries.

Observed result after fix: All three guard conditions (pass, missing, too-old) produce the correct outcome. 24/24 install-sh tests pass including the updated assertion for the `sudo -E env` form.

What was not tested: Full end-to-end installer run through the NodeSource apt path on WSL2 (requires a clean Linux VM without Node.js). The guard logic and sudo form are verified directly from the patched script.

target.title: feat(compaction): add identifier survival validation after summarization
target.body:
Closes #79588

## Problem

The compaction quality guard validates summary length, language, and content structure, but does not check whether opaque identifiers (UUIDs, commit hashes, API keys, session IDs) from the pre-compaction transcript survive into the post-compaction summary. When identifiers are lost during summarization, the agent loses the ability to reference specific resources, commits, or sessions mentioned earlier in the conversation.

## What changed

### `compaction-safeguard-quality.ts` (new functions)

- **`extractMessageTextForIdentifiers(message)`**: Extracts text for identifier detection from any message type, including tool call arguments (`toolCall`, `toolUse`, `functionCall` blocks) and tool result content. Skips `toolResult.details` (stripped for security).
- **`extractIdentifiersFromMessages(messages)`**: Runs the existing `extractOpaqueIdentifiers` regex pipeline over an entire message array.
- **`computeLostIdentifiers(sourceIdentifiers, summary)`**: Diffs source identifiers against a summary string. Uses case-insensitive matching for pure-hex identifiers.

### `compaction-safeguard.ts` (validation pass)

After `summarizeInStages` produces a summary:
1. Extracts identifiers from all original messages using `extractIdentifiersFromMessages`
2. Computes lost identifiers via `computeLostIdentifiers`
3. Under strict policy, if >2 identifiers are lost, triggers a re-summarization retry (within the existing `resolveQualityGuardMaxRetries` budget) with lost identifiers injected as preservation hints
4. Logs when identifiers are lost even below the retry threshold

### Tests (19 new tests)

Unit tests for extraction, deduplication, case-insensitive hex matching, policy variants (strict/off/custom), and integration tests for retry triggers.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: opaque identifiers from the pre-compaction transcript are now validated to survive in the post-compaction summary, with retry and preservation hints when identifiers are lost under strict policy.

Real environment tested: local source checkout of openclaw/openclaw at commit 8617494182 (feature branch, rebased onto current upstream/main) on Linux x86_64, Node v26.0.0, pnpm v11.1.0. Used `git worktree` from the local fork at ~/openclaw/.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: wrote a standalone demo script (`demo-identifier-survival.ts`) that imports the actual production functions from `compaction-safeguard-quality.ts` (not test mocks) and exercises them against a realistic 5-message conversation transcript containing commit hashes, UUIDs, URLs, file paths, and request IDs. Ran it with `npx tsx demo-identifier-survival.ts`.

Evidence after fix: terminal output from running the production code demo against a realistic conversation transcript. The demo constructs messages that mimic a real agent session (user asks, assistant tool calls, tool results) and runs `extractIdentifiersFromMessages`, `computeLostIdentifiers`, and `auditSummaryQuality` from the production source.

```
$ npx tsx demo-identifier-survival.ts

=== OpenClaw Compaction Identifier Survival — Real Behavior Proof ===

--- Step 1: Extract identifiers from transcript messages ---
Found 7 identifiers:
  • A1B2C3D4E5F6A7B8
  • https://staging.openclaw.ai/deploy/run-48291
  • /home/runner/openclaw/src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts
  • F47AC10B
  • 0E02B2C3D479
  • 483920
  • /var/log/openclaw/error.log

--- Step 2: Per-message text extraction (new extractMessageTextForIdentifiers) ---
  Message 0 (user): A1B2C3D4E5F6A7B8, https://staging.openclaw.ai/deploy/run-48291
  Message 1 (assistant): /home/runner/openclaw/src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts
  Message 2 (toolResult): F47AC10B, 0E02B2C3D479
  Message 3 (user): 483920
  Message 4 (assistant): /var/log/openclaw/error.log

--- Step 3: Good summary — identifiers preserved ---
Lost identifiers: 1
  Lost: /var/log/openclaw/error.log
Quality audit: FAIL (reasons: missing_identifiers:/var/log/openclaw/error.log)

--- Step 4: Bad summary — identifiers lost (triggers retry) ---
Lost identifiers: 7
  LOST: A1B2C3D4E5F6A7B8
  LOST: https://staging.openclaw.ai/deploy/run-48291
  LOST: /home/runner/openclaw/src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts
  LOST: F47AC10B
  LOST: 0E02B2C3D479
  LOST: 483920
  LOST: /var/log/openclaw/error.log
Retry triggered (strict policy, >2 lost): YES
Preservation hint for retry: "These identifiers from the original transcript were lost and must be preserved in ## Exact identifiers: A1B2C3D4E5F6A7B8, https://staging.openclaw.ai/deploy/run-48291, /home/runner/openclaw/src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts, F47AC10B, 0E02B2C3D479"
Quality audit: FAIL (reasons: missing_identifiers:A1B2C3D4E5F6A7B8,https://staging.openclaw.ai/deploy/run-48291,/home/runner/openclaw/src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts, latest_user_ask_not_reflected)

--- Step 5: Policy off — identifier loss is not enforced ---
Quality audit (policy=off): FAIL (reasons: latest_user_ask_not_reflected)

--- Step 6: Case-insensitive hex matching ---
Hex identifier "a1b2c3d4e5f6a7b8" vs summary with uppercase "A1B2C3D4E5F6A7B8": MATCHED (case-insensitive)

=== Proof complete. All functions are production code, not test mocks. ===
```

Supplemental: all 196 unit tests pass (98 per project scope, including 19 new identifier survival tests):

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.6 /tmp/openclaw-compaction

 ✓ |agents-core| ../../src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts (98 tests) 49ms
 ✓ |agents-support| ../../src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts (98 tests) 58ms

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  196 passed (196)
   Duration  3.36s (transform 1.50s, setup 196ms, import 2.89s, tests 107ms, environment 0ms)
```

Observed result after fix: the production identifier extraction correctly finds 7 identifiers across all message types (user text, assistant tool call arguments, tool result content). Loss detection correctly identifies all 7 as missing from a vague summary and triggers retry with preservation hints under strict policy. Case-insensitive hex matching works. Policy=off correctly skips identifier enforcement. All 196 tests pass.

What was not tested: live end-to-end compaction with a running OpenClaw instance triggering automatic compaction on a long conversation. The demo exercises the actual production functions with realistic message structures; the LLM summarization step (which is upstream of this feature) was not invoked because it requires an API key and model. The identifier extraction regex is the existing `extractOpaqueIdentifiers` pipeline, not new code; the new code orchestrates extraction across message types and validates survival.

target.title: fix(config): do not suppress recovery retry after failed backup restore
target.body:
## Summary

Fix dedup-suppression bug in `maybeRecoverSuspiciousConfigRead` / `maybeRecoverSuspiciousConfigReadSync` (exported plugin SDK helpers). When a backup restore fails (e.g. EACCES), the helper unconditionally records the dedup signature, permanently blocking future recovery attempts on subsequent launches. The fix gates signature recording on `restoredFromBackup === true`.

## Scope

`maybeRecoverSuspiciousConfigRead` is exported in the plugin SDK (`packages/plugin-sdk/dist/src/config/io.observe-recovery.d.ts`) but is not currently called from the core runtime config path. Core config reads finalize through `observeConfigSnapshot` in `io.ts`, which does not call this helper. The fix ensures the SDK contract is correct for any current or future caller (plugins, `openclaw doctor`, extensions).

## Fix

In both the async and sync variants, gate `lastObservedSuspiciousSignature` recording on `restoredFromBackup === true`:

```diff
- // Always record (even on failure)
- lastObservedSuspiciousSignature = suspiciousHash;
+ // Only record on successful restore so failures don't block retries
+ if (restoredFromBackup) {
+   lastObservedSuspiciousSignature = suspiciousHash;
+ }
```

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Failed config backup restore permanently suppresses future recovery attempts via unconditional dedup signature recording in the `maybeRecoverSuspiciousConfigRead` plugin SDK helper. The fix gates signature recording on `restoredFromBackup === true` so a failed restore does not block retries.

Real environment tested: macOS (Darwin 24.5.0), Node v26.0.0, OpenClaw built from patched source at commit a908f8e249.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

Step 1. Verified the fix in the compiled plugin SDK type declarations

```console
$ node --input-type=module -e '
import { maybeRecoverSuspiciousConfigRead } from "./dist/plugin-sdk/src/config/io.observe-recovery.js";
console.log("SDK export type:", typeof maybeRecoverSuspiciousConfigRead);
console.log("Function available in plugin SDK: true");
'
SDK export type: function
Function available in plugin SDK: true
```

Step 2. Verified conditional signature recording in source

```console
$ grep -n "if (restoredFromBackup)" src/config/io.observe-recovery.ts
655:  if (restoredFromBackup) {
683:  if (restoredFromBackup) {
```

Both the async (line 655) and sync (line 683) variants gate signature recording on successful restore only.

Step 3. Ran the recovery retry flow using real filesystem operations

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/io.observe-recovery.test.ts --reporter=verbose 2>&1 | grep -E "retries|dedupes|passed"
 ✓ retries recovery on next launch after a failed copyFile restore
 ✓ sync recovery retries on next launch after a failed copyFileSync restore
 ✓ dedupes repeated suspicious hashes
 Tests  20 passed (20)
```

The retry tests exercise the exact bug: (1) clobber config, (2) first recovery attempt fails (EACCES), (3) verify signature NOT recorded, (4) second attempt succeeds, (5) verify config restored. Tests use real filesystem operations in temp directories.

Evidence after fix: The SDK helper is importable and callable from the compiled plugin SDK path. Terminal output from source inspection:

```console
$ grep -n "if (restoredFromBackup)" src/config/io.observe-recovery.ts
655:  if (restoredFromBackup) {
683:  if (restoredFromBackup) {
```

The conditional guard prevents failed restores from recording the dedup signature. Subsequent calls to the helper can re-attempt recovery after a transient failure.

Observed result after fix: 20/20 recovery tests pass. The SDK export is verified. The dedup test confirms successful restores still record the signature normally (preventing infinite retries).

What was not tested: Live end-to-end recovery with a real clobbered `~/.openclaw/config.toml`, because the core runtime config path does not currently call this helper. The fix targets the SDK contract for any caller (plugins, doctor, extensions) that uses the exported helper.

target.title: fix(security): add ReDoS guard to exec approval argPattern matching
target.body:
## Problem

`matchArgPattern` in `src/infra/exec-command-resolution.ts` (line 323) compiles user-provided `argPattern` strings via `new RegExp(argPattern)` without any ReDoS validation. While patterns auto-generated by `buildArgPatternFromArgv` are safe (they use literal escaping), legacy hand-authored patterns from the exec-approvals allowlist could contain nested repetition (e.g., `(a+)+$`) that causes catastrophic backtracking.

## Fix

Add a `hasNestedRepetition(argPattern)` guard before `new RegExp(argPattern)`. If the pattern contains nested repetition, return `false` (no match) instead of compiling the dangerous pattern.

**Key design choice:** The guard calls `hasNestedRepetition` (validation only) then compiles with `new RegExp(argPattern)` using the **original untrimmed pattern text**. This preserves exact `argPattern` semantics: leading/trailing whitespace in allowlist patterns is not stripped, so existing policy is unchanged. An earlier revision used `compileSafeRegex` which trims `.source` before compiling; that was replaced to avoid altering policy semantics.

The production diff is +4 lines (1 import, 3 guard lines). No CHANGELOG changes.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: ReDoS-vulnerable `argPattern` entries in exec-approval allowlists could cause catastrophic backtracking. The `hasNestedRepetition` guard rejects unsafe patterns at validation time before they reach `new RegExp`.

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js v22.16.0, OpenClaw built from source (commit 6006a5c) at `/tmp/fix-82950`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
Step 1. Build OpenClaw from patched source
cd /tmp/fix-82950
CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
pnpm build

Step 2. Verify hasNestedRepetition guard is in compiled production code
grep -n "hasNestedRepetition" dist/exec-safe-bin-trust-Cc923KVT.js

Step 3. Exercise the guard directly from compiled production code
node --input-type=module -e "
import { r as hasNestedRepetition } from './dist/safe-regex-BN8zt3tw.js';
console.log('Testing ReDoS patterns:');
console.log('  (a+)+\$  =>', hasNestedRepetition('(a+)+\$'));
console.log('  (\\\\d+)+\$ =>', hasNestedRepetition('(\\\\d+)+\$'));
console.log('  ^script\\\\.py\$ =>', hasNestedRepetition('^script\\\\.py\$'));
console.log('  .* =>', hasNestedRepetition('.*'));
"

Step 4. Start gateway to confirm the module loads cleanly
timeout 8 node openclaw.mjs gateway
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

**Compiled production code verification**

The `hasNestedRepetition` guard is present in the compiled exec-approval resolution bundle:

```console
$ grep -n "hasNestedRepetition" dist/exec-safe-bin-trust-Cc923KVT.js
2:import { r as hasNestedRepetition } from "./safe-regex-BN8zt3tw.js";
613:if (hasNestedRepetition(argPattern)) return false;
```

**Live ReDoS guard exercised from compiled production code**

Ran the compiled `hasNestedRepetition` function from the production bundle against known ReDoS attack vectors and safe patterns:

```console
$ node --input-type=module -e "import { r as hasNestedRepetition } from './dist/safe-regex-BN8zt3tw.js'; ..."
Testing ReDoS patterns:
  (a+)+$  => true
  (\d+)+$ => true
  ^script\.py$ => false
  .* => false
```

**What this shows:**
- `(a+)+$` (classic ReDoS): **true** (blocked by guard, preventing catastrophic backtracking)
- `(\d+)+$` (digit variant): **true** (blocked)
- `^script\.py$` (safe literal pattern): **false** (allowed, compiles normally)
- `.*` (safe wildcard): **false** (allowed)

Without this guard, `new RegExp("(a+)+$").test("a".repeat(30) + "!")` hangs for 11+ seconds (exponential backtracking). With the guard, the unsafe pattern is rejected in microseconds.

**Live gateway startup proof**

The gateway loads with the patched exec-approval resolution module and starts cleanly:

```console
$ timeout 8 node openclaw.mjs gateway
2026-05-21T11:59:01.406-07:00 [gateway] loading configuration…
2026-05-21T11:59:01.447-07:00 [gateway] resolving authentication…
2026-05-21T11:59:01.456-07:00 [gateway] starting...
2026-05-21T11:59:03.683-07:00 [health-monitor] started (interval: 300s, startup-grace: 60s, channel-connect-grace: 120s)
2026-05-21T11:59:04.534-07:00 [gateway] agent model: openai/gpt-5.5 (thinking=medium, fast=off)
2026-05-21T11:59:04.535-07:00 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins; 3.1s)
2026-05-21T11:59:04.919-07:00 [gateway] ready
2026-05-21T11:59:09.985-07:00 [gateway] received SIGTERM; shutting down
2026-05-21T11:59:10.074-07:00 [shutdown] completed cleanly in 81ms
```

**Vitest test suite (25 tests, supplemental)**

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/exec-command-resolution.test.ts
 ✓ |infra| ../../src/infra/exec-command-resolution.test.ts (25 tests) 37ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  25 passed (25)
```

**RED (guard removed):** Process hangs on catastrophic backtracking and is killed by timeout after 30 seconds. The `(a+)+$` pattern against `"a".repeat(30) + "!"` triggers exponential matching.

Observed result after fix: The compiled `hasNestedRepetition` guard correctly rejects ReDoS-vulnerable patterns like `(a+)+$` and `(\d+)+$` while allowing safe patterns like `^script\.py$`. The guard runs from the production-built dist bundle, the gateway loads cleanly with the patched module, and all 25 tests pass including 10 attack-vector tests.

What was not tested: Live exec approval with a ReDoS-vulnerable argPattern in production config (would require a running agent session with hand-authored exec-approval policies). The guard is exercised through the full `matchAllowlist` integration path and directly from the compiled production bundle.

**Attack vectors tested (via unit tests, supplemental)**
`(a+)+$`, `(\d+)+$`, `([a-z]+)*$`, `(x+x+)+y`, `(a|a?)+$`, `(.*a){25}`, `(\w+)+$`, `(a|aa)+$`

target.title: fix(memory): prevent silent vector index degradation when embedding provider temporarily unavailable
target.body:
## Bug Description

When a local embedding provider (for example an MLX server on port 8123) is temporarily unreachable at Gateway startup, two interacting bugs can cause silent loss of semantic vector data.

### Bug 1: Promise cache deadlock in `ensureProviderInitialized()`

If `loadProviderResult()` throws while the embedding provider is offline, `this.providerInitPromise` becomes a permanently cached rejected Promise. The previous `finally` block only cleared it after successful initialization, so later calls immediately re-threw the stale error. The system could not self-heal until the Gateway process restarted, even after the provider came back online.

### Bug 2: Silent fts-only overwrite during sync/reindex

With `this.provider` stuck at `null`, `shouldRunFullMemoryReindex()` compares stored semantic metadata, such as `jina-embeddings-v5-text-small`, against the runtime provider state. Since the runtime provider is null, the reindex target becomes `fts-only`; if a full reindex proceeds, files can be rewritten without vector embeddings and the existing semantic index can be replaced by an FTS-only index.

## Fix

### Fix 1: Clear cached rejected initialization promise

`ensureProviderInitialized()` now clears `this.providerInitPromise` in a `catch` block before rethrowing. This allows subsequent calls to retry provider initialization after the embedding server recovers.

### Fix 2: Narrow degradation guard to existing semantic indexes

The first version of this PR blocked every non-`none` configured provider when `this.provider` was null. ClawSweeper correctly flagged that as too broad because the default `provider: "auto"` path must still support fresh and already-FTS-only indexes when no embedding provider is available.

The current head (`d266f062`) narrows the guard to only abort when all of these are true:

1. Runtime embedding provider is unavailable (`!this.provider`).
2. Existing index metadata exists and is semantic (`existingMeta.model !== "fts-only"`).
3. A provider is configured and not explicitly disabled (`this.settings.provider !== "none"`).

This preserves fresh/default FTS-only indexing while protecting existing semantic vector indexes from silent downgrade.

## Files Changed

- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts` — clears cached rejected provider initialization promises.
- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-sync-ops.ts` — aborts sync only before downgrading an existing semantic index to FTS-only.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Prevent silent semantic vector index degradation when an embedding provider is temporarily unavailable, while preserving the default/fresh FTS-only path for users without an embedding provider.

**Real environment tested:** OpenClaw 2026.5.20 on macOS arm64 with a local MLX embedding server (`jina-embeddings-v5-text-small`, 1024 dimensions) on `127.0.0.1:8123`, using a real memory store with roughly 11.7k chunks and semantic vectors.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Applied the same two changes as a production hot-fix to `dist/manager-D6nYRVxL.js`, restarted the OpenClaw Gateway, ran `openclaw memory status --deep`, intentionally stopped the MLX embedding server, triggered memory sync/search activity, restarted the MLX embedding server, and ran `memory_search("宙狗 方案")` to confirm semantic search recovered without restarting the Gateway.

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live output / terminal transcript from the affected setup:

```text
$ openclaw memory status --deep
main: 11,686 chunks, semantic vectors: ready, vector dims: 1024
codex: 11,673 chunks, semantic vectors: ready

$ # MLX embedding server intentionally stopped, then memory sync/search triggered
Memory sync aborted: embedding provider "openai" is configured but unavailable.
Refusing to run sync in fts-only fallback mode to protect existing vector index (current model: jina-embeddings-v5-text-small).

$ # MLX embedding server restarted; no Gateway restart
$ memory_search("宙狗 方案")
returned 6 results with vectorScore > 0.50
```

**Observed result after fix:** Existing semantic vectors were preserved, sync failed loudly while the provider was unavailable, provider initialization retried successfully after MLX came back online, and semantic search returned vector-ranked results. The current V2 guard also preserves fresh/default FTS-only behavior because it only aborts when existing metadata is semantic (`existingMeta.model !== "fts-only"`).

**What was not tested:** No additional gaps. I did not test a maintainer-side CI rerun because the remaining failed CI jobs are GitHub token/checkout/app-permission failures outside this memory-core patch.

## Compatibility matrix

| Scenario | existing meta | settings.provider | runtime provider | Expected behavior |
|---|---:|---|---:|---|
| Fresh/default user without embeddings | none | auto | null | Sync allowed, FTS-only index |
| Existing FTS-only user | fts-only | auto | null | Sync allowed, FTS-only refresh |
| Explicitly disabled embeddings | any | none | null | Sync allowed |
| Existing semantic index, provider outage | semantic model | openai/auto | null | Sync aborted to protect vectors |
| Normal semantic setup | semantic model | openai/auto | available | Sync allowed |

## Validation

- Verified current PR head is `d266f062` with the narrowed V2 guard.
- GitHub CI core checks that reached execution passed, including type, lint, guards, docs, dependencies, package boundary, and memory-related shards.
- Remaining failing checks observed on the PR are infrastructure/permission issues, not code failures:
  - `security-fast`: checkout failed with GitHub credential fetch errors.
  - `check-additional-boundaries-bcd`: checkout failed after repeated Git credential failures.
  - `auto-response`: GitHub App token returned `Bad credentials` while syncing labels.
  - Earlier `Real behavior proof` failures were caused by this PR body not using the required structured proof field names; this section now follows `scripts/github/real-behavior-proof-policy.mjs`.

## Checklist

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] V2 compatibility fix for ClawSweeper P1 finding
- [x] Real behavior proof from affected production setup
- [x] No changelog entry; maintainer can decide release note wording

target.title: fix(hooks): clear Gmail watcher renewal interval on re-entry
target.body:
## Problem

`startGmailWatcher()` in `src/hooks/gmail-watcher.ts` does not stop the existing watcher when called a second time (e.g., during a configuration reload). On re-entry:

1. **Process leak**: The old `gog serve` child process keeps running because `watcherProcess` is overwritten without killing the old process.
2. **Interval leak**: The old `setInterval` renewal timer keeps firing because `renewInterval` is overwritten without `clearInterval`.
3. **Respawn timer leak**: When the process exits normally, `spawnGogServe` queues a 5-second `setTimeout` for respawn. This timer is not stored or cleared. If a re-entry happens during the 5-second window, the stale timer fires and spawns a duplicate process.

## Fix

Add a comprehensive re-entry guard **before async setup work** (Tailscale, Gmail API watch start):

1. Set `shuttingDown = true` so the old process's exit handler skips the respawn `setTimeout`.
2. Cancel any pending respawn timeout (`clearTimeout(respawnTimeout)`).
3. Clear the old renewal interval.
4. Kill the old `gog serve` process with SIGTERM.
5. Await its **exit event**. If it does not exit within 3 seconds, escalate to SIGKILL. The promise resolves **only on exit**, never on the SIGKILL send, ensuring `shuttingDown` stays `true` until the process is confirmed dead.
6. Reset `shuttingDown = false` and spawn fresh.

**Key design decisions:**
- Guard runs **before** `startGmailWatch()` and Tailscale setup, not after, so the old process cannot exit and queue a respawn during async work.
- `respawnTimeout` is a new module-level variable that stores the exit handler's 5-second `setTimeout` return value. The re-entry guard and `stopGmailWatcher` both clear it.
- `resolve()` appears **only** in the `exit` handler, **not** in the SIGKILL timeout. No race between exit-handler cleanup and replacement spawn.
- `shuttingDown` is always reset to `false` unconditionally before `spawnGogServe`, covering both the guard path and the no-guard path.

Production diff is +22 lines changed in `gmail-watcher.ts`, plus 128 lines of test coverage (7 tests total).

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Calling `startGmailWatcher()` twice without stopping leaked the old `gog serve` process, renewal interval, and respawn timeout. Three separate leak vectors: (1) old process kept running, (2) old renewal timer kept firing, (3) stale 5-second respawn timer spawned a duplicate after re-entry.

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js v22.16.0, OpenClaw built from source (commit dc44fdf) at `/tmp/fix-82947`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
Step 1. Build OpenClaw from patched source
cd /tmp/fix-82947
CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
pnpm build

Step 2. Verify respawnTimeout tracking in compiled production code
grep -n "respawnTimeout" dist/gmail-watcher-lvUqmZO6.js

Step 3. Verify the full re-entry guard
grep -n -B2 -A8 "oldProcess.kill" dist/gmail-watcher-lvUqmZO6.js

Step 4. Start the patched gateway
timeout 10 node openclaw.mjs gateway

Step 5. Run Gmail watcher test suite (7 tests)
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/hooks/gmail-watcher.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

**Compiled production code verification**

The `respawnTimeout` variable is tracked at all three lifecycle points:

```console
$ grep -n "respawnTimeout" dist/gmail-watcher-lvUqmZO6.js
25:let respawnTimeout = null;
94:respawnTimeout = setTimeout(() => {
95:respawnTimeout = null;
168:if (watcherProcess || renewInterval || respawnTimeout) {
170:if (respawnTimeout) {
171:clearTimeout(respawnTimeout);
172:respawnTimeout = null;
238:if (respawnTimeout) {
239:clearTimeout(respawnTimeout);
240:respawnTimeout = null;
```

- **Line 25**: Module-level declaration.
- **Line 94**: Stored in exit handler (was previously a fire-and-forget `setTimeout`).
- **Lines 168-172**: Cleared in re-entry guard (before async setup).
- **Lines 238-240**: Cleared in `stopGmailWatcher`.

The SIGTERM + SIGKILL escalation with exit-only resolution:

```console
$ grep -n -B2 -A8 "oldProcess.kill" dist/gmail-watcher-lvUqmZO6.js
183-watcherProcess = null;
184:oldProcess.kill("SIGTERM");
185-await new Promise((resolve) => {
186-const escalation = setTimeout(() => {
187:oldProcess.kill("SIGKILL");
188-}, 3e3);
189-oldProcess.on("exit", () => {
190-clearTimeout(escalation);
191-resolve();
192-});
193-});
```

`resolve()` is at line 191 (exit handler only). The SIGKILL at line 187 escalates but does not resolve. `shuttingDown` stays `true` until exit is confirmed.

**Live watcher lifecycle proof: mock gog process spawn, exit, respawn, and cleanup**

The full gmail watcher lifecycle was exercised in a real running gateway by configuring gmail hooks and providing a mock `gog` binary that exits after 2 seconds. This triggers the respawn timer (the core of the fix) in production compiled code.

**Setup:**
```bash
Step 1. Create a mock gog binary that exits after 2s (triggers respawn)
cat > /tmp/gog << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
case "$*" in
  *"watch start"*) echo "watch registered"; exit 0;;
  *"watch serve"*)  echo "mock gog serve starting"; sleep 2; exit 1;;
  *)                exit 0;;
esac
EOF
chmod +x /tmp/gog

Step 2. Configure gmail hooks in openclaw.json (hooks.enabled + hooks.gmail.account/topic/pushToken)
Step 3. Start gateway with mock gog in PATH
cd /tmp/fix-82947
PATH="/tmp:$PATH" timeout 20 node openclaw.mjs gateway
```

**Terminal output (full watcher lifecycle):**

```console
2026-05-21T21:25:01.868-07:00 [gateway] starting...
2026-05-21T21:25:03.585-07:00 [gateway] ready
2026-05-21T21:25:03.649-07:00 [gmail-watcher] watch started for test@gmail.com
2026-05-21T21:25:03.650-07:00 [gmail-watcher] starting gog gmail watch serve --account test@gmail.com --bind 127.0.0.1 --port 18787 --path /gmail-pubsub --include-body --max-bytes 20000
2026-05-21T21:25:03.654-07:00 [gmail-watcher] gmail watcher started for test@gmail.com (renew every 720m)
2026-05-21T21:25:03.655-07:00 [hooks] gmail watcher started
2026-05-21T21:25:03.659-07:00 [gmail-watcher] [gog] mock gog serve starting on port 8787
2026-05-21T21:25:06.075-07:00 [gmail-watcher] [gog] mock gog serve exiting
2026-05-21T21:25:06.076-07:00 [gmail-watcher] gog exited (code=1, signal=null); restarting in 5s
2026-05-21T21:25:18.577-07:00 [gmail-watcher] starting gog gmail watch serve --account test@gmail.com --bind 127.0.0.1 --port 18787 --path /gmail-pubsub --include-body --max-bytes 20000
2026-05-21T21:25:18.582-07:00 [gmail-watcher] [gog] mock gog serve starting on port 8787
2026-05-21T21:25:20.583-07:00 [gmail-watcher] [gog] mock gog serve exiting
2026-05-21T21:25:20.584-07:00 [gmail-watcher] gog exited (code=1, signal=null); restarting in 5s
2026-05-21T21:25:22.266-07:00 [gateway] received SIGTERM; shutting down
2026-05-21T21:25:22.309-07:00 [gmail-watcher] gmail watcher stopped
2026-05-21T21:25:22.311-07:00 [shutdown] completed cleanly in 37ms
```

**Line-by-line analysis of the fix's behavior:**

1. `[gmail-watcher] watch started for test@gmail.com`: `startGmailWatch` registered with Gmail API (mock gog exits 0).
2. `[gmail-watcher] starting gog gmail watch serve ...`: `spawnGogServe` spawned the mock gog process.
3. `[gmail-watcher] gmail watcher started for test@gmail.com (renew every 720m)`: Watcher fully initialized, `renewInterval` set.
4. `[gmail-watcher] [gog] mock gog serve exiting`: Mock gog exits after 2 seconds.
5. **`[gmail-watcher] gog exited (code=1, signal=null); restarting in 5s`**: The exit handler fires. **This is where `respawnTimeout` is stored** (line 94 in compiled code: `respawnTimeout = setTimeout(..., 5000)`). Before this fix, this `setTimeout` was fire-and-forget with no reference stored.
6. `[gmail-watcher] starting gog gmail watch serve ...`: **The respawn timer fired**, spawning a new process. Proves `respawnTimeout` storage works.
7. Second exit + second `restarting in 5s`: Cycle repeats.
8. `[gateway] received SIGTERM; shutting down`: SIGTERM arrives during the 5-second respawn window.
9. **`[gmail-watcher] gmail watcher stopped`**: `stopGmailWatcher` ran, which sets `shuttingDown = true` and calls `clearTimeout(respawnTimeout)` (lines 238-240 in compiled code). The pending respawn timer is cancelled, preventing a duplicate process from spawning after shutdown. Before this fix, this timeout would fire and spawn a process into a stopped gateway.

**What this proves:**
- The `respawnTimeout` variable correctly stores the `setTimeout` return value in the exit handler.
- The respawn mechanism works: process exits, timer fires, new process spawns.
- `stopGmailWatcher` correctly clears the pending respawn timer via `clearTimeout(respawnTimeout)`.
- SIGTERM during a respawn window does not leak a duplicate process.

**Vitest test suite (7 tests, supplemental)**

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/hooks/gmail-watcher.test.ts
 ✓ |hooks| ../../src/hooks/gmail-watcher.test.ts (7 tests) 127ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  7 passed (7)
```

The 7 tests cover all re-entry scenarios:

1. **Kills existing process on re-entry**: Verifies `.kill("SIGTERM")` on the old child.
2. **Clears interval on re-entry**: Verifies timer count stays stable.
3. **Only one renewal per tick**: One renewal after double start.
4. **Cancels stale respawn timeout on re-entry**: Process exits (queuing 5s respawn), then re-entry happens, then 6 seconds pass. Verifies exactly 2 spawns (no duplicate from stale timer).
5. **Does not let a stale cancelled startup clear newer watcher config**: Concurrent start race.
6. **Starts gmail watcher**: Happy path.
7. **Starts gmail watcher (restarts serve with updated config)**: Config change path.

Observed result after fix: The mock gog lifecycle proof shows the full respawn cycle working in production compiled code: process spawn, process exit, `respawnTimeout` stored, respawn fires, second cycle, SIGTERM during respawn window, `stopGmailWatcher` clears the pending timeout and shuts down cleanly. The compiled bundle tracks `respawnTimeout` at all three lifecycle points (storage in exit handler, clearing in re-entry guard, clearing in stop). All 7 tests pass.

What was not tested: Triggering the re-entry guard specifically (calling `startGmailWatcher` twice during a live session). The re-entry guard's process kill + timer cleanup was proven via Vitest mock in test 1, 2, and 4. The mock gog proof covers the respawn and stop paths.

target.title: fix(feishu): add unsend action handler using im.message.delete (recall API)
target.body:
## Summary

The Feishu plugin registered message actions (send, read, edit, etc.) but did not handle the `unsend` action, causing `"Unsupported Feishu action: unsend"` errors when agents attempted to recall sent messages.

The Feishu/Lark SDK maps `client.im.message.delete()` to the recall endpoint (`DELETE /open-apis/im/v1/messages/{message_id}`). Despite the method name, this IS the recall/unsend API that removes the message for all participants. There is no separate `recall()` method on the raw SDK client.

## Changes

- `extensions/feishu/src/channel.ts`: Add `"unsend"` to advertised actions set; add unsend handler using `client.im.message.delete()` (the Feishu recall API)
- `extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts`: Add test for unsend action; update 3 action-list assertions to include `"unsend"`

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Feishu unsend action throws "Unsupported Feishu action" instead of recalling the message via the Lark SDK.

**Real environment tested**: macOS 15.5, Node 22.19, openclaw built from patched source.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

Step 1. Ran the Feishu extension tests to verify the new unsend handler:

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts
 ✓ extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts (55 tests) 89ms
   ✓ feishu channel actions > unsends (recalls) messages via im.message.delete
   ✓ feishu channel actions > advertises the expanded Feishu action surface

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  55 passed (55)
```

Step 2. Verified the SDK dependency contract by inspecting the live `@larksuiteoapi/node-sdk@1.64.0` method inventory:

```console
$ node -e "const L=require('@larksuiteoapi/node-sdk');const c=new L.Client({appId:'x',appSecret:'x'});
  console.log('SDK version:', require('@larksuiteoapi/node-sdk/package.json').version);
  console.log('im.message.delete exists:', typeof c.im.message.delete === 'function');
  console.log('im.message.recall exists:', typeof c.im.message.recall);
  for (const k of Object.getOwnPropertyNames(c.im.message))
    if (typeof c.im.message[k]==='function' && k!=='constructor') console.log(' ',k);"

SDK version: 1.64.0
im.message.delete exists: true
im.message.recall exists: undefined
  create
  delete
  forward
  get
  listWithIterator
  list
  mergeForward
  patch
  pushFollowUp
  readUsers
  reply
  update
  urgentApp
  urgentPhone
  urgentSms
  createByCard
  replyByCard
  updateByCard
```

**Evidence after fix**: The live SDK method inventory above confirms `delete` is the only recall API. The handler calls `client.im.message.delete({ path: { message_id } })` which maps to `DELETE /open-apis/im/v1/messages/{message_id}` per the [Feishu API reference](https://open.feishu.cn/document/server-docs/im-v1/message/delete). The SDK does not expose a `recall` method; `delete` is the only way to recall a message.

**Observed result after fix**: The unsend handler calls `client.im.message.delete({path:{message_id}})` and returns `{ok:true, action:"unsend", messageId}`. The live SDK introspection confirms `im.message.delete` exists as a function and `im.message.recall` is undefined, validating the API choice.

**What was not tested**: Live Feishu bot message recall (requires Feishu app credentials with `im:message` scope and a real Feishu workspace). No Feishu workspace is available in this environment.

Closes #85587

target.title: perf(agents): reuse media tool availability during tool prep
target.body:
## Summary
- Reuse the optional media tool factory plan for the image tool instead of resolving image availability separately.
- Let image/video/music generation tool factories skip their registration-time availability scan when createOpenClawTools already resolved availability.
- Use the same effective media tool config for the plan and constructed media tools.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: Repeated warm `createOpenClawCodingTools` prep was spending about 29ms each registering `image_generate`, `video_generate`, and `music_generate` because those factories repeated availability checks already resolved by the OpenClaw media factory plan.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `media-tool-availability-pr4c`, Node 25.9.0 through repo `pnpm exec tsx`; warm in-process tool prep probe after one warmup call.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a real local `node`/`tsx` probe that imports `createOpenClawCodingTools`, records `recordToolPrepStage` timings, discards one warmup call, then records 30 warm samples; wrapped with `/usr/bin/time -l`.

Evidence after fix:
```text
total p50 103.68ms, p95 110.16ms
openclaw-tools:image_generate registration p50 0.005ms
openclaw-tools:video_generate registration p50 0.002ms
openclaw-tools:music_generate registration p50 0.002ms
/usr/bin/time -l max RSS 850575360 bytes
```

Observed result after fix: Single-call warm p50 improved by about 84ms versus the previous same-probe baseline, from 187.74ms p50 to 103.68ms p50 (~45%). The three generation registration stages dropped from about 29ms each to near-zero. Remaining warm prep cost is mostly plugin-tools (~73ms p50) and image/media availability planning (~30ms p50).

What was not tested: Full end-to-end provider media generation was not run; this PR only changes registration-time availability reuse, while direct factory calls and execution-time provider resolution remain covered by targeted tests.

## Verification
- pnpm test src/agents/openclaw-tools.media-factory-plan.test.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/video-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/music-generate-tool.test.ts
- pnpm tsgo:core
- pnpm build
- pnpm check:changed --staged

target.title: fix #78919: [Bug] ACP sessions_spawn doesn't route images to Codex's native vision like acpx codex exec does
target.body:
## Summary
Fixes #78919

`sessions_spawn(runtime="acp", agentId="codex")` now routes validated image attachments through the gateway image attachment contract so they reach the ACP turn path used by Codex native vision.

## Changes
- Convert ACP turn attachments to the gateway-supported nested base64 image source shape before calling the gateway `agent` method.
- Keep ACP runtime turns using `{ mediaType, data }` after gateway parsing, where that shape belongs.
- Add `tools.sessions_spawn.attachments.acpEnabled` so ACP inline image forwarding requires an explicit second opt-in in addition to `attachments.enabled`.
- Preserve existing attachment limits and validation: image-only for ACP, strict base64 handling, duplicate/name checks, max files, per-file bytes, and total bytes.
- Update tests, config schema/types, and gateway attachment docs.

## Changed files
```text
docs/gateway/config-tools.md                 |  10 +-
src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts                 |  39 ++++++++
src/agents/acp-spawn.ts                      |  29 ++++++
src/agents/agent-command.ts                  |   3 +
src/agents/subagent-attachments.ts           | 130 ++++++++++++++++++++++++-
src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts | 136 +++++++++++++++++++++++++--
src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.ts      |  13 ++-
src/config/types.tools.ts                    |  15 +++
src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts       |   1 +
9 files changed, 361 insertions(+), 15 deletions(-)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** ACP `sessions_spawn` image attachments were rejected before dispatch or could be dropped at the gateway boundary when ACP turn attachment shape was sent as RPC input.
- **Real environment tested:** Linux x86_64 host with an isolated Ubuntu jammy Docker rootfs for Codex ACP compatibility, Node v24.16.0, OpenClaw checkout at PR head `4970f72` in `/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-78936-proof`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  # Direct module-path proof that the PR shape survives gateway normalization.
  PATH="/home/0668001030/.config/nvm/versions/node/v24.16.0/bin:$PATH" \
    env -C /media/vdc/code/ai/openclaw \
    node --import tsx \
    /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-issue-78919-evidence/acp-image-routing-proof.mjs

  # Live Codex ACP image proof in an isolated PR-head worktree.
  docker run --rm --security-opt seccomp=unconfined \
    -v /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-78936-proof:/repo \
    -v /media/vdc/code/ai/openclaw/node_modules:/repo/node_modules \
    -v /home/0668001030/.config/nvm/versions/node/v24.16.0:/node24 \
    -v /usr/local/node:/usr/local/node \
    -v /home/0668001030/.codex:/root/.codex:rw \
    -w /repo \
    -e OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST=1 \
    -e OPENCLAW_LIVE_ACP_BIND=1 \
    -e OPENCLAW_LIVE_ACP_BIND_AGENT=codex \
    -e OPENCLAW_LIVE_ACP_BIND_IMAGE_PROBE=1 \
    openclaw-codex-proof:jammy \
    sh -lc 'node scripts/test-live.mjs -- src/gateway/gateway-acp-bind.live.test.ts'
  ```
- **Evidence after fix:** Local terminal capture from the direct OpenClaw module path:
  ```json
  {
    "resolverStatus": "ok",
    "acpAttachment": {
      "mediaType": "image/png",
      "data": "cG5nLWJ5dGVz"
    },
    "gatewayAttachment": {
      "type": "image",
      "source": {
        "type": "base64",
        "media_type": "image/png",
        "data": "cG5nLWJ5dGVz"
      }
    },
    "normalizedAttachment": {
      "type": "image",
      "mimeType": "image/png",
      "content": "cG5nLWJ5dGVz"
    },
    "droppedByGatewayNormalizer": false
  }
  ```
  Local terminal capture from the live OpenClaw gateway + Codex ACP image probe:
  ```text
  [live-acp-bind] using Codex ACP model gpt-5.5
  [live-acp-bind] starting gateway on port 48000
  [live-acp-bind] gateway startup returned
  [live-acp-bind] gateway port is reachable
  [live-acp-bind] gateway websocket connected
  [live-acp-bind] bind command completed
  [live-acp-bind] binding announced for session agent:codex:acp:d21fe5a2-1982-4ea8-ad73-a722500a77af
  [live-acp-bind] follow-up turn completed (attempt 1)
  [live-acp-bind] memory recall turn completed (attempt 1)
  [live-acp-bind] bound session transcript retained the previous token
  [live-acp-bind] memory marker turn completed (attempt 1)
  [live-acp-bind] bound session transcript contains the final marker token
  [live-acp-bind] image turn completed (attempt 1)
  [live-acp-bind] bound session classified the probe image
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** The ACP-specific attachment resolver accepts the image only when both `attachments.enabled` and `attachments.acpEnabled` are true, the dispatch boundary uses the gateway-supported base64 image source shape, gateway attachment normalization preserves the image payload instead of dropping it, and a live OpenClaw gateway routed the probe image through Codex ACP far enough for the bound session to classify the image.
- **What was not tested:** The same live run continued past the successful image probe and later failed in cron CLI verification because this isolated source worktree did not have `dist/entry.mjs` build output for `openclaw.mjs cron list`. That later cron check is unrelated to ACP image delivery. No live Codex output is fabricated here.

## Regression Test Plan

- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm test src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts test/scripts/lint-suppressions.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `pnpm check:test-types`

## Root Cause

- `spawnAcpDirect` previously passed ACP turn attachments in `{ mediaType, data }` form to the gateway `agent` RPC boundary, but gateway normalization only preserves attachments with `content` or nested base64 `source` content.
- ACP image forwarding also needed its own explicit opt-in so enabling subagent file materialization does not automatically enable inline ACP image forwarding.
