target.title: fix(agent): prevent sticky model fallback
target.body:
## Summary
- roll back temporary auto fallback model overrides after successful fallback attempts
- keep session routing pointed at the selected model while still recording usage/cost for the actual fallback model used
- update runner/session accounting coverage and add the Unreleased changelog entry

## Root cause
Successful fallback candidates were persisted into session override state but only rolled back on errors/classified failures. Usage persistence also wrote `providerUsed`/`modelUsed` into the session identity, so one fallback run could pin later turns to the smaller fallback model even after the primary model recovered.

## Validation
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/session.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: A temporary model fallback should not make the session stick to the fallback model. After a primary-model rate limit sends one turn to a configured fallback, the next turn in the same session should try the configured primary again while usage/status still records the actual fallback runtime used for the fallback turn.

Real environment tested: Local source checkout at PR head `c4f5337a7fdcc9f11c17ff6f42d8e8295fb77eed`, Node `v24.15.0`, pnpm `11.1.0`, isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME=/var/tmp/openclaw-pr75270-proof/home`, isolated workspace `/var/tmp/openclaw-pr75270-proof/workspace`, and a local OpenAI-compatible HTTP endpoint on `127.0.0.1`. No real user session, live channel, or production OpenClaw home was used.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: 1. Configured `agents.defaults.model.primary` as `primary-local/primary-model` with `fallbacks: ["fallback-local/fallback-model"]`.
2. Started a local OpenAI-compatible `/v1/chat/completions` server that returns four HTTP 429 responses for `primary-model`, then returns streaming success for `fallback-model`; after that, `primary-model` returns streaming success.
3. Ran two local embedded agent turns against the same session id:

`/usr/bin/node openclaw.mjs agent --local --agent main --session-id pr75270-proof --thinking off --json --timeout 120 --message <proof message>`

Evidence after fix: Copied terminal output from `/var/tmp/openclaw-pr75270-proof/proof-output.json`:

```json
{
  "first": {
    "exitCode": 0,
    "text": "PR75270 fallback proof OK",
    "provider": "fallback-local",
    "model": "fallback-model",
    "fallbackAttempts": [
      {
        "provider": "primary-local",
        "model": "primary-model",
        "error": "429 proof primary rate limit",
        "reason": "rate_limit",
        "status": 429
      }
    ],
    "requestModels": [
      "primary-model",
      "primary-model",
      "primary-model",
      "primary-model",
      "fallback-model",
      "fallback-model"
    ],
    "storeAfterTurn": {
      "modelProvider": "fallback-local",
      "model": "fallback-model",
      "providerOverride": null,
      "modelOverride": null,
      "modelOverrideSource": null,
      "fallbackNoticeSelectedModel": null,
      "fallbackNoticeActiveModel": null
    }
  },
  "second": {
    "exitCode": 0,
    "text": "PR75270 primary recovered OK",
    "provider": "primary-local",
    "model": "primary-model",
    "fallbackAttempts": [],
    "requestModels": [
      "primary-model",
      "primary-model"
    ],
    "storeAfterTurn": {
      "modelProvider": "primary-local",
      "model": "primary-model",
      "providerOverride": null,
      "modelOverride": null,
      "modelOverrideSource": null,
      "fallbackNoticeSelectedModel": null,
      "fallbackNoticeActiveModel": null
    }
  },
  "allRequestModels": [
    "primary-model",
    "primary-model",
    "primary-model",
    "primary-model",
    "fallback-model",
    "fallback-model",
    "primary-model",
    "primary-model"
  ]
}
```

The runtime log also showed the fallback decision on the first turn:

```text
[model-fallback/decision] model fallback decision: decision=candidate_failed requested=primary-local/primary-model candidate=primary-local/primary-model reason=rate_limit next=fallback-local/fallback-model detail=429 proof primary rate limit
[model-fallback/decision] model fallback decision: decision=candidate_succeeded requested=primary-local/primary-model candidate=fallback-local/fallback-model reason=unknown next=none
```

Observed result after fix: The first turn used the configured fallback after the synthetic primary returned 429, and `fallbackAttempts` recorded the primary failure. The same session's persisted auto override fields were clear after the fallback turn. The second turn, with the primary endpoint recovered, requested and completed on `primary-local/primary-model` with no fallback attempts. This verifies the session did not stay pinned to the fallback model.

What was not tested: This proof used a local OpenAI-compatible endpoint rather than a paid hosted provider, did not exercise real Telegram/WhatsApp delivery, and did not test provider-specific OAuth rotation. Those paths are covered separately by the automated tests listed above.

target.title: fix(browser): thread snapshot timeoutMs through agent tool and helpers
target.body:
Fixes #72934.

## Summary

- Problem: `browser action=snapshot` consistently times out on macOS (issue #72934). `open` / `navigate` succeed, but every snapshot path times out — making the browser tool effectively unusable from agents.
- Why it matters: the snapshot timeout passed by callers (and the implicit 20_000 ms default) is silently dropped at multiple layers, so neither the agent-supplied `timeoutMs` nor any sane default reaches the Chrome MCP / Playwright snapshot helpers.
- What changed: thread one normalized `timeoutMs` from the agent tool input through `browserSnapshot` (client) → `BrowserSnapshotPlan` (`/snapshot` route plan) → `/snapshot` route → Chrome MCP `take_snapshot` / `take_screenshot` (labels) and Playwright `snapshotAi` / `snapshotRole` / `snapshotAria` / `screenshotWithLabels` helpers. Add `DEFAULT_BROWSER_SNAPSHOT_TIMEOUT_MS = 20_000` and replace the previously hardcoded `20000` literal so the default value is unchanged. The default is now applied uniformly at the `executeSnapshotAction` boundary so it reaches both the direct client call and the proxied path; without this, the proxy path was effectively unbounded.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): act / click / navigate paths (already fixed in PR #69733 / #63524); Linux/Ubuntu fault path #72653 (likely a different root cause and explicitly out of scope per the bot triage); Chrome MCP / Playwright internals beyond passing `timeoutMs` to existing race / API options; `extensions/browser/` boundary is preserved; docs/tools/browser.md is not touched. (CHANGELOG.md includes the required browser snapshot timeout fix entry, added in a separate follow-up commit per the Clawsweeper guidance.)

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #72934
- Related #69733 (`navigateChromeMcpPage` timeout race — directly mirrored here for `take_snapshot`), #72653 (Linux/all-action timeout — different root cause, out of scope)
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the snapshot path in `extensions/browser/` does not propagate `timeoutMs` end-to-end. Specifically: `executeSnapshotAction` never reads `input.timeoutMs` (and never injects the default), so the proxied snapshot request reaches the gateway with no `timeoutMs` at all; `browserSnapshot` (client) hardcodes `timeoutMs: 20000` and has no `opts.timeoutMs`; `BrowserSnapshotPlan` has no `timeoutMs` field; `takeChromeMcpSnapshot` calls `callTool` without forwarding any `options.timeoutMs`, so the Promise.race + session reset added in PR #69733 never engages for snapshot; `snapshotRoleViaPlaywright` / `snapshotAriaViaPlaywright` ignore caller timeouts (the role helper hardcodes 5000 ms; the aria helper has no timeout at all on `Accessibility.getFullAXTree`).
- Missing detection / guardrail: there are no targeted tests asserting that an explicit `timeoutMs` from the agent tool reaches each snapshot helper; `browser-tool.test.ts` mocked `browserSnapshot` and never inspected timeout-related arguments.
- Contributing context (if known): PR #69733 added the `timeoutMs` race to `callTool` and used it for `navigate_page`, but did not extend the same wiring to `take_snapshot` or to the Playwright snapshot helpers — leaving the snapshot path silently subject to whatever the underlying Chrome MCP / Playwright operation chooses to do.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this: [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/client.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/routes/agent.snapshot.plan.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.snapshot.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: an explicit `timeoutMs` supplied by an agent must reach `browserSnapshot` (and the `/snapshot` query string), `BrowserSnapshotPlan`, `takeChromeMcpSnapshot` (where it must arm the `callTool` race), `snapshotRoleViaPlaywright` (must reach `page.ariaSnapshot({ timeout })`), `snapshotAriaViaPlaywright` (must race `Accessibility.getFullAXTree`), and the proxied browser snapshot path. The `DEFAULT_BROWSER_SNAPSHOT_TIMEOUT_MS = 20_000` default is also locked in for both direct and (implicitly via `executeSnapshotAction`) proxied paths.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: each new test asserts one seam; together they cover every layer where `timeoutMs` is dropped today, without requiring a real Chromium / Playwright session.
- Existing test that already covers this: none — `browser-request.timeout.test.ts` covers `browser.request` local dispatches but not snapshot path; `agent.snapshot.test.ts` covers `resolveTargetIdAfterNavigate` only.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A (10 new test cases added across 5 files in this PR).

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Default snapshot timeout is unchanged in **value** (still 20_000 ms; the hardcoded literal is replaced by `DEFAULT_BROWSER_SNAPSHOT_TIMEOUT_MS = 20_000`). What does change is **enforcement scope**: the same 20_000 ms default that previously only applied to direct host snapshots now applies uniformly to the proxied node-target snapshot path as well — previously the proxy path silently had no per-request timeout for snapshot, leaving it subject to whatever Chrome MCP / Playwright happened to do. Caller-supplied `timeoutMs` is now honored end-to-end across all snapshot paths. No public schema change beyond `BrowserSnapshotPlan` gaining an optional `timeoutMs` field.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
agent input.timeoutMs ─┐
                       └─► executeSnapshotAction (drops timeoutMs)
                          └─► proxyRequest / browserSnapshot
                              └─► /snapshot route
                                  ├─► takeChromeMcpSnapshot (no timeoutMs → callTool race never armed)
                                  ├─► snapshotAiViaPlaywright (caller never passes timeoutMs)
                                  ├─► snapshotRoleViaPlaywright (no param; hardcoded 5000 ms)
                                  └─► snapshotAriaViaPlaywright (no param; unbounded getFullAXTree)
result: snapshot consistently times out, no actionable error, browser tool unusable

After:
agent input.timeoutMs ─► executeSnapshotAction (defaults to DEFAULT_BROWSER_SNAPSHOT_TIMEOUT_MS = 20_000)
                       ─► proxyRequest({ query: { timeoutMs }, timeoutMs }) / browserSnapshot({ timeoutMs })
                          └─► /snapshot route reads plan.timeoutMs
                              ├─► takeChromeMcpSnapshot({ timeoutMs }) → callTool race + session reset
                              ├─► snapshotAiViaPlaywright({ timeoutMs })
                              ├─► snapshotRoleViaPlaywright({ timeoutMs }) → page.ariaSnapshot({ timeout })
                              └─► snapshotAriaViaPlaywright({ timeoutMs }) → Accessibility.getFullAXTree raced
result: one normalized snapshot timeout reaches every layer; default value unchanged at 20_000 ms.
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (developer worktree, Ubuntu headless); upstream issue #72934 reports macOS Darwin 25.4.0 arm64
- Runtime/container: Node 22, vitest 4.x
- Model/provider: N/A (timeout propagation is provider-agnostic)
- Integration/channel (if any): browser plugin
- Relevant config (redacted): default browser config

### Steps

1. Run focused tests: `pnpm test extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/client.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/routes/agent.snapshot.plan.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.snapshot.test.ts`.
2. Inspect new tests:
   - `client.test.ts` — `browserSnapshot` writes `timeoutMs` into the `/snapshot?` query (explicit value + 20_000 default).
   - `agent.snapshot.plan.test.ts` — `resolveSnapshotPlan` parses `timeoutMs` and rejects non-positive values.
   - `chrome-mcp.test.ts` — `takeChromeMcpSnapshot({ timeoutMs: 75 })` rejects with the `take_snapshot` race error after fake timers advance.
   - `browser-tool.test.ts` — `executeSnapshotAction` passes `input.timeoutMs` to both the direct `browserSnapshot` call and the proxied `callBrowserProxy` (`params.query.timeoutMs` + `params.timeoutMs`); falls back to the default 20_000 ms when none is supplied.
   - `pw-tools-core.snapshot.test.ts` — `snapshotAriaViaPlaywright({ timeoutMs: 750 })` races `Accessibility.getFullAXTree` and rejects on timeout; `snapshotRoleViaPlaywright({ refsMode: "aria", timeoutMs: 8888 })` forwards the value into `page.ariaSnapshot({ mode: "ai", timeout: 8888 })`.
3. Targeted formatter + lint coverage applies to the touched files (oxfmt + repo lint wrappers).

### Expected

- All targeted tests pass; the explicit `timeoutMs` is observable at every snapshot seam, and the default reaches both the direct and proxied paths.

### Actual

- 95 / 95 tests pass across the 5 focused files.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after — implicit (the new tests would have failed before this change since `timeoutMs` was never threaded through any of the asserted seams; e.g., the `chrome-mcp.test.ts` snapshot race test would never reject because `takeChromeMcpSnapshot` did not pass `options.timeoutMs` to `callTool`).
- [x] Real-behavior trace — see `## Real behavior proof` section below.
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Real behavior proof

Captured 2026-05-17 against branch `pr/openclaw-browser-snapshot-timeout-propagation` head `6646e1edb9` (= 3 commits on top of `e6c1a6637a`).

**Behavior or issue addressed:** the `browser action=snapshot` path used to ignore agent-supplied `timeoutMs` at every layer below the agent tool, so a slow or hung Chrome MCP / Playwright snapshot could only be cut short by the global request timeout. This PR threads `timeoutMs` from the agent tool input, through `executeSnapshotAction`, through the `browserSnapshot` HTTP client, through the `/snapshot` route plan resolver, through `takeChromeMcpSnapshot`, and through both Playwright helpers (`snapshotAriaViaPlaywright` Promise.race wrapper + `snapshotRoleViaPlaywright` Playwright `ariaSnapshot({ timeout })`) all the way to the leaf accessibility call. The Codex review asked for evidence that the parameter is honored by a real boundary rather than only by unit tests.

**Real environment tested:** Linux x86_64 local dev workspace (Node 22.22.2, pnpm 10.33.2). A real loopback Node `http.createServer` stands in for the OpenClaw browser agent's `/snapshot` route with a controlled per-request delay. The actual production `browserSnapshot` HTTP client from this PR's source (`extensions/browser/src/browser/client.ts`) is invoked against that real socket, so `fetchBrowserJson` exercises its real AbortController and the URL query carries the real serialized `timeoutMs`. Live Chromium was attempted but blocked on missing `libasound.so.2` in this sandbox (cannot apt-install without sudo); the HTTP-client boundary is the next outermost real seam and is the one the agent tool actually reaches in production.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

1. Start a real Node loopback HTTP server with a configurable delay.
2. Import the production `browserSnapshot` client from `extensions/browser/src/browser/client.ts` and call it with `{ format: "ai", timeoutMs: X }` against the loopback server.
3. Vary `(timeoutMs, server delay)`: baseline (no `timeoutMs`, fast server), tight `timeoutMs` against a slow server (expect abort near `timeoutMs`), and loose `timeoutMs` against a moderate server (expect success).
4. Capture client-side elapsed time, observed outcome (success / `timed out after Xms`), and the URL query the server actually received.

Concrete commands run on the branch:

```
$ OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/proof-openclaw-home \
    node node_modules/.bin/tsx scripts/proof-browser-snapshot-timeout.ts
$ CI=true pnpm test \
    extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts \
    extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts \
    extensions/browser/src/browser/client.test.ts \
    extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.snapshot.test.ts \
    extensions/browser/src/browser/routes/agent.snapshot.plan.test.ts
$ CI=true pnpm tsgo:extensions
```

**Evidence after fix:**

Terminal transcript from the HTTP-boundary probe (real Node socket, no mocks):

```
=== environment ===
Node:                    v22.22.2
HTTP boundary:           real loopback Node http.createServer with controlled delay
browserSnapshot import:  extensions/browser/src/browser/client.ts (this PR's source)
why not real Chromium:   headless shell missing libasound.so.2 in this env

=== baseline: no timeoutMs (default 20000ms), server delays 50ms ===
  input timeoutMs:       undefined (server delay: 50ms)
  elapsed_ms:            78.9
  outcome:               ok
  payload.targetId:      "live-test-target"
  payload.snapshot:      "h1 Snapshot timeout proof\nbutton Primary action\n"
  payload.stats.nodes:   17
  server saw request:    GET /snapshot
  server saw timeoutMs:  20000
  server saw format:     ai

=== explicit timeoutMs=9000, server delays 50ms (should succeed) ===
  input timeoutMs:       9000 (server delay: 50ms)
  elapsed_ms:            57.5
  outcome:               ok
  payload.targetId:      "live-test-target"
  payload.snapshot:      "h1 Snapshot timeout proof\nbutton Primary action\n"
  payload.stats.nodes:   17
  server saw request:    GET /snapshot
  server saw timeoutMs:  9000
  server saw format:     ai

=== explicit timeoutMs=300, server delays 2000ms (should abort on timeout) ===
  input timeoutMs:       300 (server delay: 2000ms)
  elapsed_ms:            310.0
  outcome:               threw (Error): Can't reach the OpenClaw browser control service (timed out after 300ms). If this is a sandboxed session, ensure the sandbox browser is running.
  server saw request:    GET /snapshot
  server saw timeoutMs:  300
  server saw format:     ai

=== explicit timeoutMs=1500, server delays 2500ms (should abort on timeout) ===
  input timeoutMs:       1500 (server delay: 2500ms)
  elapsed_ms:            1505.4
  outcome:               threw (Error): Can't reach the OpenClaw browser control service (timed out after 1500ms). If this is a sandboxed session, ensure the sandbox browser is running.
  server saw request:    GET /snapshot
  server saw timeoutMs:  1500
  server saw format:     ai

=== explicit timeoutMs=4000, server delays 500ms (should succeed) ===
  input timeoutMs:       4000 (server delay: 500ms)
  elapsed_ms:            506.6
  outcome:               ok
  payload.targetId:      "live-test-target"
  payload.snapshot:      "h1 Snapshot timeout proof\nbutton Primary action\n"
  payload.stats.nodes:   17
  server saw request:    GET /snapshot
  server saw timeoutMs:  4000
  server saw format:     ai

=== done ===
```

Focused-test summary on the rebased branch (5 files, 95 cases):

```
$ CI=true pnpm test \
    extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts \
    extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts \
    extensions/browser/src/browser/client.test.ts \
    extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.snapshot.test.ts \
    extensions/browser/src/browser/routes/agent.snapshot.plan.test.ts

 Test Files  5 passed (5)
      Tests  95 passed (95)
   Duration  2.66s
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 6.01s
```

**Observed result after fix:**

| input `timeoutMs` | server delay | client-side elapsed | outcome |
|-------------------|--------------|--------------------|---------|
| (none) | 50ms | 78.9ms | ok (URL carried default `timeoutMs=20000`) |
| 9000 | 50ms | 57.5ms | ok (URL carried `timeoutMs=9000`) |
| **300** | **2000ms** | **310.0ms** | **threw `timed out after 300ms`** |
| **1500** | **2500ms** | **1505.4ms** | **threw `timed out after 1500ms`** |
| 4000 | 500ms | 506.6ms | ok (URL carried `timeoutMs=4000`) |

What this proves at the public HTTP-client boundary:

- The agent-tool `timeoutMs` reaches the URL query of `GET /snapshot?...&timeoutMs=X`, so the downstream `/snapshot` route handler (already covered by `agent.snapshot.plan.test.ts`) has the value it needs to feed to `takeChromeMcpSnapshot` / `snapshotAriaViaPlaywright` / `snapshotRoleViaPlaywright`.
- The client-side AbortController fires at the requested `timeoutMs`, not at the legacy default. Wall-clock elapsed tracks the input within ~10 ms (310ms for 300ms, 1505ms for 1500ms), and the thrown message names the exact value (`timed out after 300ms`, `timed out after 1500ms`), so a hung downstream cannot keep the agent waiting longer than the agent asked.
- Both the request payload (`timeoutMs=X` in the URL query) and the response payload (the snapshot JSON: `targetId`, `snapshot`, `stats`) cross a real socket round-trip.

Combined with the 95 focused unit cases that already cover the downstream layers (`/snapshot` plan resolution, Chrome MCP `callTool` race, Playwright `ariaSnapshot({ timeout })` forwarding, Promise.race wrapper for the Accessibility CDP call), the whole agent-input → leaf-call chain is now observably timeoutMs-honoring.

**What was not tested:**

- Live Chromium / real Playwright `ariaSnapshot` on a real page — blocked on missing `libasound.so.2` in this sandbox (apt-install requires sudo, not available here). The PR's Playwright leaf is covered by the in-tree vitest cases (`pw-tools-core.snapshot.test.ts` — including the `races against an explicit timeoutMs` and `forwards an explicit timeoutMs into the role-aria Playwright ariaSnapshot call` cases) and the wrapper logic is mechanically the same as the already-merged `navigateChromeMcpPage` model from PR #69733.
- macOS Linux 路 #72653 (separately tracked, out of scope for this PR).
- `pnpm check:changed` typecheck-all picked up two pre-existing typecheck errors in `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/handler.ts` (`Property 'dmScope' does not exist`, `Cannot find name 'liveDmAllowFrom'`) that are introduced by upstream `extensions/matrix/` advances after this PR's base `e6c1a6637a` (`git log e6c1a6637a..upstream/main -- extensions/matrix/` lists 3 upstream commits, none touched by this PR). The browser-scoped focused tests above remain green.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - Direct `browser action=snapshot` path: `executeSnapshotAction` forwards `input.timeoutMs` to `browserSnapshot({ timeoutMs })` and the resulting `/snapshot?` URL carries the value (default 20_000 when absent).
  - Proxied path through `callBrowserProxy`: the gateway invocation receives `params.query.timeoutMs` AND `params.timeoutMs` so both the proxied HTTP request and the Chrome MCP race honor it (assertion locks in `outer = inner + 5_000 ms slack`).
  - Chrome MCP layer: `takeChromeMcpSnapshot` arms the `callTool` race, matching the `navigateChromeMcpPage` model from PR #69733.
  - Playwright helpers: `snapshotRoleViaPlaywright` forwards `timeoutMs` to `page.ariaSnapshot({ timeout })` / `locator.ariaSnapshot({ timeout })`; `snapshotAriaViaPlaywright` races the unbounded `Accessibility.getFullAXTree` call.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Missing / non-positive `timeoutMs` values fall through to the existing 20_000 ms default — covered by the `agent.snapshot.plan.test.ts` regression for `0` and `not-a-number`, and by the `client.test.ts` / `browser-tool.test.ts` "default snapshot timeout" assertions.
  - Optional `BrowserSnapshotPlan.timeoutMs` field — additive, so existing plan consumers remain unaffected; verified via the focused snapshot tests (5 files / 95 tests pass).
  - The Playwright helpers clamp `timeoutMs` to Playwright's accepted range (500–60_000 ms). This matches the existing `snapshotAiViaPlaywright` model (`page.ariaSnapshot({ mode: "ai", timeout: Math.max(500, Math.min(60_000, ...))})`) and is preserved here as defensive normalization for the same external-API guarantees, not a new policy added in this PR.
- What you did **not** verify: manual browser smoke not run; test environment is Ubuntu headless and has no prior validated browser-extension smoke setup. End-to-end snapshot reproduction against a real Chromium / Chrome MCP / Playwright stack was not executed. The fix is justified by targeted tests that exercise each seam where `timeoutMs` was dropped before this change.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`
- Default snapshot timeout value preserved at 20_000 ms; `BrowserSnapshotPlan.timeoutMs` is an optional field, so plan consumers (`shouldUsePlaywrightForAriaSnapshot` etc.) remain non-breaking. Direct snapshot path keeps the same effective deadline; the proxied path now also honors that deadline rather than being silently unbounded — this is the bug the PR fixes.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `page.ariaSnapshot({ timeout })` / `locator.ariaSnapshot({ timeout })` rely on the Playwright Locator API supporting an explicit timeout option.
  - Mitigation: the same option is already used in production by `snapshotAiViaPlaywright` (`page.ariaSnapshot({ mode: "ai", timeout })`), so the Playwright surface is known to accept it.
- Risk: forwarding `timeoutMs` to `takeChromeMcpSnapshot` engages the `callTool` race + session reset, which could surface previously masked transport hangs.
  - Mitigation: this is the intended behavior — without the race, snapshot hangs were silent; the same wiring landed for `navigate_page` in PR #69733 without follow-up issues.
- Risk: applying the 20_000 ms default to the proxied path introduces a per-request deadline where there previously was none, which could surface timeouts that previously hung indefinitely.
  - Mitigation: this is the bug fix itself — the symptom in #72934 is exactly that the snapshot path hangs forever; surfacing the deadline at 20_000 ms (the same value the direct path already uses) restores intended parity.

## Notes for maintainers

- A CHANGELOG.md entry was added under `## Unreleased` → `### Fixes` in a separate follow-up commit, per Clawsweeper's required-changelog guidance; please relocate or rephrase at release time if desired.
- This PR keeps the default `DEFAULT_BROWSER_SNAPSHOT_TIMEOUT_MS = 20_000`. Adjusting that default is a separate UX discussion and intentionally not in this PR.
- The Linux/Ubuntu all-action timeout reported in #72653 is explicitly out of scope; even if the snapshot path on macOS recovers with this fix, #72653 likely needs a different root-cause investigation (Playwright direct connection also fails per the issue thread).
- The Playwright helpers preserve the existing `Math.max(500, Math.min(60_000, ...))` clamp around `page.ariaSnapshot({ timeout })` (matching `snapshotAiViaPlaywright`). This is a defensive normalization for what Playwright accepts, not a new policy introduced by this PR.

target.title: [perf] improve reply send performance
target.body:
## Summary
- improve reply latency by avoiding dispatcher-idle waits on every direct block reply callback
- flush pending block delivery only before subsequent tool/progress events, preserving block-before-tool ordering
- keep ACP direct block replies non-blocking while preserving visible reply chronology
- schedule source reply transcript mirroring after successful `message.action` sends without blocking the gateway response
- serialize async source transcript mirrors per session so same-session transcript chronology is preserved
- send Telegram direct-message typing once access has passed, before later context/session construction can delay the indicator, while avoiding a duplicate immediate cue

## Root Cause
Recent reply-path changes moved delivery bookkeeping into hot paths for lightweight replies: block callbacks waited for dispatcher idle, and `message.action` waited for transcript mirroring after platform delivery. Under concurrent embedded runs, cron work, or slow channel/transcript I/O, this amplified reply latency and event-loop starvation. The 36-54h window also includes Telegram dispatch replay dedupe changes that added work before the existing typing cue path.

## Impact
Simple visible replies can return sooner without compromising user-visible send order. The existing block-before-tool/progress ordering guarantee is preserved by flushing only when later progress is about to be emitted. Transcript mirroring remains best-effort and still runs after successful source-conversation sends, with per-session ordering to avoid mirrored transcript reordering. Telegram direct typing starts earlier after authorization without sending an extra duplicate initial cue.

## Validation
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/send.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.typing.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.typing.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.typing.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

target.title: perf(agents): reuse media tool availability during tool prep
target.body:
## Summary
- Reuse the optional media tool factory plan for the image tool instead of resolving image availability separately.
- Let image/video/music generation tool factories skip their registration-time availability scan when createOpenClawTools already resolved availability.
- Use the same effective media tool config for the plan and constructed media tools.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: Repeated warm `createOpenClawCodingTools` prep was spending about 29ms each registering `image_generate`, `video_generate`, and `music_generate` because those factories repeated availability checks already resolved by the OpenClaw media factory plan.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `media-tool-availability-pr4c`, Node 25.9.0 through repo `pnpm exec tsx`; warm in-process tool prep probe after one warmup call.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a real local `node`/`tsx` probe that imports `createOpenClawCodingTools`, records `recordToolPrepStage` timings, discards one warmup call, then records 30 warm samples; wrapped with `/usr/bin/time -l`.

Evidence after fix:
```text
total p50 103.68ms, p95 110.16ms
openclaw-tools:image_generate registration p50 0.005ms
openclaw-tools:video_generate registration p50 0.002ms
openclaw-tools:music_generate registration p50 0.002ms
/usr/bin/time -l max RSS 850575360 bytes
```

Observed result after fix: Single-call warm p50 improved by about 84ms versus the previous same-probe baseline, from 187.74ms p50 to 103.68ms p50 (~45%). The three generation registration stages dropped from about 29ms each to near-zero. Remaining warm prep cost is mostly plugin-tools (~73ms p50) and image/media availability planning (~30ms p50).

What was not tested: Full end-to-end provider media generation was not run; this PR only changes registration-time availability reuse, while direct factory calls and execution-time provider resolution remain covered by targeted tests.

## Verification
- pnpm test src/agents/openclaw-tools.media-factory-plan.test.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/video-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/music-generate-tool.test.ts
- pnpm tsgo:core
- pnpm build
- pnpm check:changed --staged

target.title: [AI-assisted] Fix duplicated acp server args in ACP client
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: `openclaw acp client` always prefixes the spawned server command with `acp`, so if an upstream caller also passes `serverArgs=["acp", ...]`, the effective command becomes `openclaw acp acp ...` and fails.
- Why it matters: this makes ACP client startup brittle and causes session creation to fail for callers that already include the subcommand in forwarded args.
- What changed: normalize `serverArgs` in the ACP client by dropping one leading `acp` when present, then prepend the canonical `acp` subcommand exactly once; added a regression test for this case.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no changes to ACP server behavior, permissions, routing, session semantics, or any non-ACP CLI paths.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

ACP client startup is now tolerant of callers that redundantly include `acp` in forwarded `serverArgs`.
Before: `openclaw acp client --server openclaw --server-args acp --server-verbose` could spawn `openclaw acp acp --verbose` and fail.
After: the same invocation normalizes to `openclaw acp --verbose` and proceeds to session creation.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

This changes ACP client command construction only by deduping a redundant leading `acp` token in forwarded server args.
Risk is low because the change narrows an invalid invocation into the canonical one instead of expanding execution surface.
Mitigation: added a regression test covering the duplicated-`acp` case.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local host runtime
- Model/provider: openai-codex/gpt-5.4
- Integration/channel (if any): ACP CLI
- Relevant config (redacted): default local OpenClaw CLI setup; no special ACP config required for repro

### Steps

1. Run `openclaw acp client --server openclaw --server-args acp --server-verbose`
2. Observe the spawned server command
3. Attempt session creation

### Expected

- The ACP client should spawn the canonical server command once as `openclaw acp --verbose`
- Session creation should proceed successfully

### Actual

- Before the fix, this could spawn `openclaw acp acp --verbose`
- That failed with `error: too many arguments for 'acp'`

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Before:

```text
[acp-client] spawning: openclaw acp acp --verbose
error: too many arguments for 'acp'
```

After:

```text
[acp-client] spawning: openclaw acp --verbose
[acp-client] creating session
```

Targeted test:

```text
npx vitest run src/acp/client.test.ts
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
    - Reproduced the failing duplicated-acp invocation locally
    - Verified the same invocation now spawns openclaw acp --verbose
    - Verified it reaches session creation successfully
- Ran npx vitest run src/acp/client.test.ts
- Edge cases checked:
    - callers that already pass leading acp in serverArgs
    - existing --server-verbose behavior remains intact after normalization
- What you did **not** verify:
    - full repository green on pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test
    - broader ACP server behavior outside this argument-normalization path

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes)
- Config/env changes? (No)
- Migration needed? (No)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR / commit
- Files/config to restore: src/acp/client.ts, src/acp/client.test.ts
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
    - ACP client spawning malformed commands
    - regression where valid serverArgs are incorrectly altered

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: overly broad normalization could accidentally rewrite intended server args
    - Mitigation: normalization only removes a single leading acp; all other args are preserved verbatim
- Risk: reviewers may assume the full repository test/build matrix is green
    - Mitigation: this PR explicitly reports targeted verification only; local pnpm build and pnpm test currently fail due to unrelated existing repository issues outside this change

target.title: feat(subagents): forward toolsAllow from sessions_spawn
target.body:
## Summary
- Add optional `toolsAllow` to `sessions_spawn` for native subagent runs.
- Validate the value as a string array, preserving explicit `[]` as an empty allowlist.
- Forward the allowlist through `SpawnSubagentParams` into the child `agent` Gateway request so the existing embedded-runner allowlist and tool construction path apply.
- Reject `toolsAllow` for `runtime="acp"` because ACP does not use the embedded-runner tool construction path.
- Reject `toolsAllow` for CLI-backed native runs because CLI harnesses cannot enforce OpenClaw embedded-runner tool allowlists.
- Document the user-facing option and add an Unreleased changelog entry.

## Change Type (select all)
- [x] Feature

## Scope (select all touched areas)
- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] Documentation

## Linked Issue/PR
- Related #78381
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)
- Behavior or issue addressed: `sessions_spawn` callers can now pass a narrow native subagent tool allowlist with `toolsAllow`; the value is exposed in the live tool schema and forwarded through the child Gateway `agent` path into the existing native embedded-runner allowlist filtering.
- Real environment tested: macOS 26.2, Node v25.9.0, pnpm 10.33.2, local OpenClaw source checkout from branch `sessions-spawn-tools-allow-pr2`.
- PR head tested: `09471b37572d88702c57190bc9c0cdfccc303385`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: rebuilt dist with `pnpm build`, then used an isolated temp `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, loopback token-auth Gateway, temp workspace, temp mock OpenAI `/v1/responses` server, and no live remote model/provider call. The same child Gateway `agent` path that `sessions_spawn` forwards into was run three times: without `toolsAllow`, with `toolsAllow: ["read", "exec"]`, and with `toolsAllow: []`.
- Evidence after fix: the mock OpenAI server captured the actual `tools` array sent by the embedded runner. Baseline sent 21 tools; the allowlisted run sent only `exec` and `read`; the explicit empty allowlist run sent 0 tools. Current-head focused tests also prove CLI-backed native runs reject `toolsAllow` before dispatch for both `["read"]` and `[]`, and explicit empty embedded `toolsAllow` uses the minimal prompt path without the skills catalog.

```json
{
  "head": "09471b37572d88702c57190bc9c0cdfccc303385",
  "gatewayPort": 41167,
  "mockOpenAiBaseUrl": "http://127.0.0.1:41168/v1",
  "baseline": {
    "label": "baseline",
    "runId": "pr2-proof-baseline-6262660c-a744-4330-972c-56051148dc92",
    "status": "ok",
    "wallMs": 2296,
    "toolCount": 21,
    "toolNames": [
      "edit",
      "exec",
      "image",
      "image_generate",
      "memory_get",
      "memory_search",
      "music_generate",
      "process",
      "read",
      "session_status",
      "sessions_history",
      "sessions_list",
      "sessions_send",
      "sessions_spawn",
      "sessions_yield",
      "subagents",
      "update_plan",
      "video_generate",
      "web_fetch",
      "web_search",
      "write"
    ]
  },
  "allowReadExec": {
    "label": "allow-read-exec",
    "runId": "pr2-proof-allow-read-exec-42d95c53-529a-460e-ba17-bab5ff57143b",
    "status": "ok",
    "wallMs": 440,
    "toolCount": 2,
    "toolNames": [
      "exec",
      "read"
    ]
  },
  "emptyAllow": {
    "label": "empty-allow",
    "runId": "pr2-proof-empty-allow-769c291b-1f33-4da9-afb3-644c0a1f3474",
    "status": "ok",
    "wallMs": 356,
    "toolCount": 0,
    "toolNames": []
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: final-head embedded native subagent runs enforce `toolsAllow` before the request reaches the model transport, and explicit `[]` disables tools instead of falling back to unrestricted defaults. CLI-backed native runs fail closed instead of silently ignoring the allowlist.
- Before evidence: without this PR, `sessions_spawn` had no `toolsAllow` schema entry and the spawned child Gateway `agent` request could not carry a per-spawn allowlist.
- What was not tested: a live remote model-backed spawned child run. The proof intentionally uses a mock OpenAI server to avoid real provider calls while still exercising the Gateway and embedded-runner path.

## Supplemental local timing note
The proof run also recorded local mock wall time: baseline `2296ms`, allowlisted `440ms`, empty allowlist `356ms`. This is included only as a local mock timing signal from one isolated run, not as a formal benchmark or guaranteed provider/model speedup. The primary value of this PR is correctness/control-plane narrowing; any latency win depends on tool set, runtime config, and provider behavior.

## Root Cause (if applicable)
N/A. This is additive feature plumbing over an existing embedded-runner allowlist mechanism.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
- Target test or file: `src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts`, `src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts`, `src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts`
- Scenario the tests lock in: `toolsAllow` is exposed, normalized, validates string arrays, preserves explicit `[]`, rejects ACP runtime usage, forwards through Gateway `agent`, and reaches embedded runs started by the command runner.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: this PR connects an existing embedded-runner option to `sessions_spawn`; focused tests cover the schema, validation, and forwarding contract without requiring live model behavior.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
`runtime="subagent"` callers may pass `toolsAllow` to narrow the spawned native subagent's tool set. Defaults are unchanged when the field is omitted. `toolsAllow: []` intentionally runs the child without tools. `runtime="acp"` rejects the option with a clear input error.

## Diagram (if applicable)
N/A.

## Security Impact (required)
- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: this only narrows the child tool set for native subagent runs. ACP rejects the option because it does not use this embedded tool construction path.

## Repro + Verification
### Environment
- OS: macOS 26.2
- Runtime/container: Node v25.9.0, pnpm 10.33.2, local source checkout
- Model/provider: mock OpenAI `/v1/responses`; no live remote model call
- Integration/channel: local loopback Gateway `agent` RPC and `agent.wait`
- Relevant config: temp workspace, temp state dir, token-auth loopback Gateway, mock provider `mock-openai/gpt-pr2-tools`

### Commands and checks
- `pnpm test src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts` passed (`5 files`, `186 tests`, 2 Vitest shards).
- `pnpm tsgo:core` passed.
- `pnpm lint:core` passed (`0 warnings and 0 errors`).
- `git diff --check` passed before the docs/changelog commit.
- `git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD` passed after the CLI fail-closed review-fix commit.
- `pnpm prompt:snapshots:check` printed `Prompt snapshots are current (7 files)`; the process then hung after printing and was killed.
- `pnpm build` passed before the current-head Gateway/mock proof.
- Final-head Gateway/mock proof above passed on `09471b37572d88702c57190bc9c0cdfccc303385`.

### Expected
- Omitted `toolsAllow`: default native child tool set is unchanged.
- `toolsAllow: ["read", "exec"]`: the child model request only sees `read` and `exec` tools.
- `toolsAllow: []`: the child model request sees no tools.

### Actual
- Omitted `toolsAllow`: 21 tools captured by mock OpenAI.
- `toolsAllow: ["read", "exec"]`: 2 tools captured by mock OpenAI, exactly `exec` and `read`.
- `toolsAllow: []`: 0 tools captured by mock OpenAI.

## Evidence
- [x] Gateway-backed terminal capture
- [x] Mock transport request capture
- [x] Focused tests, typecheck, lint, whitespace check

## Human Verification (required)

- Edge cases checked: omitted `toolsAllow`, explicit empty `[]`, blank strings trimmed/filtered, non-string entries rejected, non-array input rejected, ACP runtime rejected, CLI-backed native runtime rejected.
- What you did **not** verify: a live provider-backed spawned child run.

## Review Conversations
- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? `Yes`

target.title: Node run/install: add --header for WebSocket upgrade (Cloudflare Zero Trust)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `openclaw node run` and `node install` had no way to pass custom HTTP headers on the WebSocket upgrade request, causing silent 302 redirects and connection failures when the node gateway sits behind Cloudflare Zero Trust or similar auth proxies
- **Why it matters:** Teams deploying OpenClaw in Zero Trust network environments (common in enterprise) were completely blocked — the WS handshake would redirect instead of upgrading, with no workaround
- **What changed:** Added a repeatable `--header "Name: Value"` CLI flag to both `node run` and `node install`; headers are persisted to config and forwarded to the gateway WebSocket client; also supports `OPENCLAW_NODE_HEADERS`, `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_ID`, and `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET` env vars for the installed daemon service
- **What did NOT change:** No changes to gateway routing logic, auth token handling, skill execution, or any behavior for users not passing `--header`

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #42792
- Related: none

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `openclaw node run` now accepts repeatable `--header "Name: Value"` flags
- `openclaw node install` now accepts repeatable `--header "Name: Value"` flags; headers are written to config and used by the installed daemon on startup
- New env vars supported: `OPENCLAW_NODE_HEADERS` (JSON), `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_ID`, `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET`
- No behavior change for users who do not pass `--header`

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `Yes` — headers may contain auth tokens; persisted to existing node config file (same location/permissions as all other config)
- New/changed network calls? `Yes` — user-supplied headers forwarded on outbound WebSocket upgrade request
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- **Risk + mitigation:** Headers containing secrets are written to the existing node config file (not a new location). No headers are logged or surfaced in output. The `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET` env var name makes sensitivity clear to users.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.x (Apple Silicon)
- Runtime/container: Node.js v22, pnpm monorepo
- Model/provider: N/A (gateway connectivity issue, not model-specific)
- Integration/channel: Cloudflare Zero Trust (simulated via mock HTTP proxy)
- Relevant config: none required

### Steps

**1. Start a mock proxy that returns 302 if the CF header is absent:**
```js
const http = require('http');
const server = http.createServer((req, res) => {
  if (!req.headers['cf-access-client-id']) {
    res.writeHead(302, { Location: 'https://example.cloudflareaccess.com' });
    res.end();
    return;
  }
  res.writeHead(200);
  res.end();
});
server.listen(9999, () => console.log('Mock proxy listening on 9999'));
```

**2. Without fix — run in second terminal:**
```bash
pnpm openclaw node run --host localhost --port 9999
```

**3. With fix — run with headers:**
```bash
pnpm openclaw node run --host localhost --port 9999 \
  --header "CF-Access-Client-Id: test123" \
  --header "CF-Access-Client-Secret: secret456"
```

### Expected

- WS upgrade request includes supplied headers
- No 302 redirect when correct Zero Trust headers are present

### Actual (before fix)

- WS upgrade sent with no custom headers → Cloudflare returns 302 → `Unexpected server response: 302`

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets

**Before fix:**
```
Unexpected server response: 302
```

**After fix:**
```
Unexpected server response: 200
```
_(200 is expected — mock is not a real WS server; the point is the 302 redirect is gone)_

New unit tests added:
- `src/gateway/client.test.ts` — headers passed to WS constructor
- `src/daemon/program-args.test.ts` — `--header` args emitted correctly
- `src/cli/node-cli/header-args.test.ts` — parse logic including edge cases

All 895 test files, 7301 tests passing. `pnpm check` clean.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:**
  - `node run` with single `--header` → header present in WS upgrade
  - `node run` with multiple `--header` flags → all headers forwarded
  - `node run` with no `--header` → behavior identical to pre-fix
  - Mock proxy 302→200 transition confirmed end-to-end
  - `pnpm check` and `pnpm test` both clean on branch
- **Edge cases checked:**
  - Malformed header string (missing `:`) → `parseHeaderArgs` throws with clear error
  - Header value containing `:` (e.g. URLs) → splits on first colon only, value preserved
  - Empty header value → accepted (valid HTTP)
- **What I did not verify:**
  - Live Cloudflare Zero Trust environment (tested via mock proxy only)
  - `node install` daemon persistence across system restarts
  - Windows path behavior for config persistence

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — `--header` is purely additive; existing behavior unchanged
- Config/env changes? `Yes` — new optional `headers` field in node config; old configs without it continue to work
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert: remove `--header` flags from invocation, or delete `headers` key from node config file
- Files/config to restore: `~/.openclaw/node-config.json` — remove the `headers` field
- Known bad symptoms: WS connections failing after upgrade if headers are malformed; `parseHeaderArgs` throws at startup with a descriptive error

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** User accidentally persists a sensitive token to disk via `node install --header`
  - **Mitigation:** Consistent with how other sensitive config (API keys) is already handled in node config. No new attack surface; file permissions unchanged.
- **Risk:** Header injection via malformed `--header` value
  - **Mitigation:** `parseHeaderArgs` validates format; `ws` library handles header serialization — no raw string concatenation into HTTP stream.

target.title: fix(memory): prevent silent vector index degradation when embedding provider temporarily unavailable
target.body:
## Bug Description

When a local embedding provider (for example an MLX server on port 8123) is temporarily unreachable at Gateway startup, two interacting bugs can cause silent loss of semantic vector data.

### Bug 1: Promise cache deadlock in `ensureProviderInitialized()`

If `loadProviderResult()` throws while the embedding provider is offline, `this.providerInitPromise` becomes a permanently cached rejected Promise. The previous `finally` block only cleared it after successful initialization, so later calls immediately re-threw the stale error. The system could not self-heal until the Gateway process restarted, even after the provider came back online.

### Bug 2: Silent fts-only overwrite during sync/reindex

With `this.provider` stuck at `null`, `shouldRunFullMemoryReindex()` compares stored semantic metadata, such as `jina-embeddings-v5-text-small`, against the runtime provider state. Since the runtime provider is null, the reindex target becomes `fts-only`; if a full reindex proceeds, files can be rewritten without vector embeddings and the existing semantic index can be replaced by an FTS-only index.

## Fix

### Fix 1: Clear cached rejected initialization promise

`ensureProviderInitialized()` now clears `this.providerInitPromise` in a `catch` block before rethrowing. This allows subsequent calls to retry provider initialization after the embedding server recovers.

### Fix 2: Narrow degradation guard to existing semantic indexes

The first version of this PR blocked every non-`none` configured provider when `this.provider` was null. ClawSweeper correctly flagged that as too broad because the default `provider: "auto"` path must still support fresh and already-FTS-only indexes when no embedding provider is available.

The current head (`d266f062`) narrows the guard to only abort when all of these are true:

1. Runtime embedding provider is unavailable (`!this.provider`).
2. Existing index metadata exists and is semantic (`existingMeta.model !== "fts-only"`).
3. A provider is configured and not explicitly disabled (`this.settings.provider !== "none"`).

This preserves fresh/default FTS-only indexing while protecting existing semantic vector indexes from silent downgrade.

## Files Changed

- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts` — clears cached rejected provider initialization promises.
- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-sync-ops.ts` — aborts sync only before downgrading an existing semantic index to FTS-only.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Prevent silent semantic vector index degradation when an embedding provider is temporarily unavailable, while preserving the default/fresh FTS-only path for users without an embedding provider.

**Real environment tested:** OpenClaw 2026.5.20 on macOS arm64 with a local MLX embedding server (`jina-embeddings-v5-text-small`, 1024 dimensions) on `127.0.0.1:8123`, using a real memory store with roughly 11.7k chunks and semantic vectors.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Applied the same two changes as a production hot-fix to `dist/manager-D6nYRVxL.js`, restarted the OpenClaw Gateway, ran `openclaw memory status --deep`, intentionally stopped the MLX embedding server, triggered memory sync/search activity, restarted the MLX embedding server, and ran `memory_search("宙狗 方案")` to confirm semantic search recovered without restarting the Gateway.

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live output / terminal transcript from the affected setup:

```text
$ openclaw memory status --deep
main: 11,686 chunks, semantic vectors: ready, vector dims: 1024
codex: 11,673 chunks, semantic vectors: ready

$ # MLX embedding server intentionally stopped, then memory sync/search triggered
Memory sync aborted: embedding provider "openai" is configured but unavailable.
Refusing to run sync in fts-only fallback mode to protect existing vector index (current model: jina-embeddings-v5-text-small).

$ # MLX embedding server restarted; no Gateway restart
$ memory_search("宙狗 方案")
returned 6 results with vectorScore > 0.50
```

**Observed result after fix:** Existing semantic vectors were preserved, sync failed loudly while the provider was unavailable, provider initialization retried successfully after MLX came back online, and semantic search returned vector-ranked results. The current V2 guard also preserves fresh/default FTS-only behavior because it only aborts when existing metadata is semantic (`existingMeta.model !== "fts-only"`).

**What was not tested:** No additional gaps. I did not test a maintainer-side CI rerun because the remaining failed CI jobs are GitHub token/checkout/app-permission failures outside this memory-core patch.

## Compatibility matrix

| Scenario | existing meta | settings.provider | runtime provider | Expected behavior |
|---|---:|---|---:|---|
| Fresh/default user without embeddings | none | auto | null | Sync allowed, FTS-only index |
| Existing FTS-only user | fts-only | auto | null | Sync allowed, FTS-only refresh |
| Explicitly disabled embeddings | any | none | null | Sync allowed |
| Existing semantic index, provider outage | semantic model | openai/auto | null | Sync aborted to protect vectors |
| Normal semantic setup | semantic model | openai/auto | available | Sync allowed |

## Validation

- Verified current PR head is `d266f062` with the narrowed V2 guard.
- GitHub CI core checks that reached execution passed, including type, lint, guards, docs, dependencies, package boundary, and memory-related shards.
- Remaining failing checks observed on the PR are infrastructure/permission issues, not code failures:
  - `security-fast`: checkout failed with GitHub credential fetch errors.
  - `check-additional-boundaries-bcd`: checkout failed after repeated Git credential failures.
  - `auto-response`: GitHub App token returned `Bad credentials` while syncing labels.
  - Earlier `Real behavior proof` failures were caused by this PR body not using the required structured proof field names; this section now follows `scripts/github/real-behavior-proof-policy.mjs`.

## Checklist

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] V2 compatibility fix for ClawSweeper P1 finding
- [x] Real behavior proof from affected production setup
- [x] No changelog entry; maintainer can decide release note wording

target.title: fix(ui): handle empty strings with minLength constraint in config save
target.body:
Fixes #85831

## Summary

- Modified `coerceFormValues()` in `form-coerce.ts` to handle string fields with `minLength` constraint
- Empty strings with `minLength: 1` (e.g., `z.string().min(1)`) are now coerced to `undefined`, allowing schema defaults to apply during validation
- Added `minLength` and `maxLength` to `JsonSchema` type definition

## Problem

When saving config in Control UI, required string fields with `minLength` constraint (like `z.string().min(1)`) were sent as empty strings `""` instead of being unset. This prevented schema defaults from applying, causing validation errors when users left fields empty.

## Solution

Added logic to treat empty strings with `minLength` constraint as unset values:
- Empty string `""` with `minLength > 0` → returns `undefined`
- Empty string `""` with `minLength: 0` or no constraint → returns `""` as-is

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Config save in Control UI ignored schema defaults for string fields with minLength constraint
- **Real environment tested**: Node.js environment on Windows 11, OpenClaw fork (OPENCLAW_HOME=D:\TA\openclaw-test-temp)
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: 
  1. Started OpenClaw gateway
  2. Opened Control UI at http://127.0.0.1:18789
  3. Navigated to Settings → Models → Model Providers
  4. Created custom provider with baseUrl="https://api.example.com"
  5. Saved successfully
  6. Edited provider, cleared baseUrl field (empty string)
  7. Attempted to save

- **Evidence after fix**:
```terminal
10:01:18 ✓ config.set 7902ms  (save succeeded)
10:04:06 ✗ config.set 1784ms errorCode=INVALID_REQUEST 
          errorMessage=invalid config: models.providers.custom-1.baseUrl: 
          custom model providers must declare baseUrl
```

**No minLength validation error** - the empty string was correctly coerced to undefined, skipping the minLength:1 constraint. The rejection came from business logic validation ("custom providers must declare baseUrl"), not from minLength validation.

- **Observed result after fix**: Empty string with minLength constraint no longer triggers "String must contain at least 1 character(s)" error. The fix correctly converts empty strings to undefined, allowing the field to be omitted.
- **What was not tested**: Fields where empty string should be preserved (no minLength constraint) - these are covered by unit tests.

## Testing

Added 5 unit tests covering:
1. Empty string with `minLength: 1` → becomes undefined
2. Empty string without constraint → preserved as empty string
3. Non-empty string with constraint → unchanged
4. `minLength: 0` treated as no constraint
5. Nested object with empty constrained field

All tests pass (36 total in form-utils.node.test.ts).

target.title: Disable Chrome MCP telemetry watchdog by default
target.body:
## Summary

- Pass `--no-usage-statistics` when OpenClaw launches `chrome-devtools-mcp@latest`.
- Preserve explicit profile-level `mcpArgs` overrides for usage statistics.
- Add regression coverage for the default arg list, explicit opt-in, explicit opt-out dedupe, and custom-command launches.

## Motivation / root cause

`chrome-devtools-mcp` enables usage statistics by default and can spawn a detached watchdog process. OpenClaw closes the MCP SDK stdio transport, but that only owns the direct child process. Disabling usage statistics at launch prevents the detached telemetry watchdog from being created in the default OpenClaw browser automation path.

Refs #85721

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Browser automation launches Chrome DevTools MCP without leaving a detached usage-statistics watchdog behind after the MCP client is closed.

Real environment tested: macOS local checkout, Node 24.10.0, real `npx chrome-devtools-mcp@latest` process launched through `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk` `StdioClientTransport`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started a real MCP SDK `Client` with `StdioClientTransport({ command: "npx", args: buildChromeMcpArgs({ cdpUrl: "http://127.0.0.1:9" }) })`, called `client.connect()`, `client.listTools()`, then `client.close()`, waited 1.2s, and scanned `ps axo pid=,ppid=,command=` for `chrome-devtools-mcp` and `watchdog/main.js`.

Evidence after fix: Copied live console output from the real SDK stdio close/process-scan smoke:

```json
{
  "generatedArgs": [
    "-y",
    "chrome-devtools-mcp@latest",
    "--browserUrl",
    "http://127.0.0.1:9",
    "--no-usage-statistics",
    "--experimentalStructuredContent",
    "--experimental-page-id-routing"
  ],
  "transportPid": 14467,
  "remainingRelevantProcesses": [],
  "watchdogProcessesRemaining": 0
}
```

Observed result after fix: Live output shows `--no-usage-statistics` in the generated argv, `remainingRelevantProcesses: []`, and `watchdogProcessesRemaining: 0`; no `chrome-devtools-mcp` or telemetry watchdog process remained after closing the SDK client.

What was not tested: I did not run a full Chrome browser UI workflow or a Linux remote/Testbox smoke in this pass.

## Regression tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.existing-session.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.stop-running-browser.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/routes/basic.existing-session.test.ts`

## Exact commands run

- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.existing-session.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.stop-running-browser.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/routes/basic.existing-session.test.ts`
- Real MCP SDK stdio close/process-scan smoke described above.

## User-visible behavior

Existing-session browser automation should no longer accumulate detached Chrome DevTools MCP telemetry watchdog processes during normal OpenClaw-managed MCP launches.

## Compatibility

Users who explicitly want upstream usage statistics can still pass a usage-statistics arg in `browser.profiles.*.mcpArgs`; explicit args override OpenClaw's default opt-out.

## Risks

Low. The change adds one documented Chrome DevTools MCP flag to the existing argv list and keeps profile-level custom args as separate argv entries.

target.title: fix(agents/harness): route CLI backend aliases through PI for in-process compaction [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

`selectAgentHarnessDecision` in `src/agents/harness/selection.ts` treated `agentRuntime.id` strictly as a plugin harness id. CLI backend aliases (`claude-cli`, `google-gemini-cli`) are documented-valid runtime ids per the schema (see `runtime-schema.ts`: *"a registered plugin harness id such as 'codex', or a supported CLI backend alias such as 'claude-cli'"*), but they are not plugin harnesses — they are CLI backends dispatched at the request layer by `runCliAgent` in `attempt-execution.ts` and `agent-runner.runtime.ts`.

Normal turns never hit the harness selector for these runtimes because the request layer short-circuits on `isCliProvider` first. But **in-process consumers that call `selectAgentHarness` directly** — most importantly `maybeCompactAgentHarnessSession`, used by both `/compact` and the preflight memory-flush path in `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded` — would throw `MissingAgentHarnessError("claude-cli")` for any agent whose model-scoped or provider-scoped `agentRuntime.id` was a CLI backend alias.

Once a session crosses the preflight compaction threshold, that throw fires on **every inbound turn across every channel** (Telegram, webchat, SMS, `/compact`), because `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded` runs before every dispatch. The agent becomes unusable until compacted — and `/compact` itself can't compact, so recovery requires a config swap or surgical patch.

## Fix

Add a `cli_backend_routed_via_pi` branch to `selectAgentHarnessDecision` right next to the existing `implicit_plugin_unavailable_pi` (codex) fallback. When the runtime is a CLI backend alias, return the PI harness with `runtime: "pi"` stamped on the policy. The new branch is gated by `isCliRuntimeAlias()` from `model-runtime-aliases.ts`, so any future CLI backend added to `LEGACY_RUNTIME_MODEL_PROVIDER_ALIASES` is covered automatically.

This routes only the **in-process step** through PI:
- For compaction: PI does the transcript summarization. Auth still flows through the user's existing CLI OAuth via `resolveClaudeCliSyntheticAuth`.
- For normal turns: unaffected. They never reach this code path because `runAgentAttempt` in `attempt-execution.ts` and `agent-runner.runtime.ts` branch to `runCliAgent` on `isCliProvider` first.

The branch mirrors the existing codex-implicit fallback shape exactly: same `buildSelectionDecision`, same `runtime: "pi"` policy override, same candidate list. No new architecture.

## Why not a CLI-native compaction path?

The CLI backend declares `reseedFromRawTranscriptWhenUncompacted: true` for the claude-cli case (see `cli-backend.ts`), which is the binary's own internal context management — not something the harness layer can call as "summarize this transcript please." Building a CLI-native compaction backend would be a much larger architectural change. PI fallback is the smallest fix consistent with how this function already handles "this runtime can't compact in-process" cases (codex implicit).

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: With model-scoped `agentRuntime.id = "claude-cli"`, `selectAgentHarnessDecision` threw `MissingAgentHarnessError("claude-cli")` for every call. This blocked `maybeCompactAgentHarnessSession`, so once a session crossed the preflight-compaction threshold, every inbound turn on every channel (Telegram, webchat, SMS, `/compact`) failed before reaching the request-layer CLI dispatch. User-visible symptom: an unresponsive bot, with `Requested agent harness "claude-cli" is not registered.` repeating in the gateway log.

- **Real environment tested**: macOS, Apple Silicon, OpenClaw 2026.5.19 installed fresh 2 days ago. Single agent (`main`) with `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6` as primary model and model-scoped `agentRuntime: { id: "claude-cli" }`. Claude CLI binary at `~/.local/bin/claude`, authenticated via the `anthropic:claude-cli` OAuth profile. Telegram bot channel active. Session at 151% of context (1.6m / 1.0m) when the bug surfaced — preflight compaction firing on every inbound turn.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Applied the semantic equivalent of this PR's patch to the locally installed bundled selector (`/opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/selection-*.js`). The two-string-literal version of the source change in this PR — added the `cli_backend_routed_via_pi` branch in `selectAgentHarnessDecision`.
  2. `openclaw gateway restart` — new gateway PID came up, listening on configured port.
  3. Sent a regular message via Telegram to my running bot.
  4. Issued `/compact` in the same Telegram thread.
  5. Tailed `/tmp/openclaw/openclaw-$(date +%Y-%m-%d).log` filtered for `MissingAgentHarnessError`, `agent/cli-backend`, and `provider=claude-cli` lines during steps 3–4.

- **Evidence after fix**:
  - **Telegram screenshot** (cropped, redacted) showing the bot's normal reply + `/compact` completing on the same channel that was throwing on every turn before the patch: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8a97397e-22e3-47b9-93e4-4b4d2e353727 (re-attached in my proof comment below for in-thread display).
  - **Before/after gateway log excerpt** (sensitive identifiers redacted, timestamps stripped): https://github.com/user-attachments/files/28105459/proof-logs.txt — attached in my proof comment.
  - Inline excerpt from the before-fix log (sensitive fields scrubbed; 5 of 24 occurrences across `lane=main` and `lane=session:agent:main:telegram:direct:<redacted>`):

    ```
    lane task error: lane=main durationMs=<redacted> error="MissingAgentHarnessError: Requested agent harness \"claude-cli\" is not registered."
    lane task error: lane=session:agent:main:telegram:direct:<redacted> durationMs=<redacted> error="MissingAgentHarnessError: Requested agent harness \"claude-cli\" is not registered."
    ```

  - Inline excerpt from the after-fix tail (same scrubbing; representative — every post-restart Telegram turn shows the same `agent/cli-backend` activity):

    ```
    cli exec: provider=claude-cli model=sonnet promptChars=<redacted> trigger=user useResume=true session=present resumeSession=<redacted> reuse=reusable historyPrompt=present
    claude live session reuse: provider=claude-cli model=claude-sonnet-4-6
    claude live session turn: provider=claude-cli model=claude-sonnet-4-6 durationMs=<redacted> rawLines=<redacted>
    ```

- **Observed result after fix**:
  - Telegram normal turn replied successfully (the bot answered the prompt I gave it instead of failing the lane).
  - `/compact` returned `Compacted • Context 37k/1.0m (3%)` as a Telegram system message — context dropped from 151% to 3%.
  - Zero `MissingAgentHarnessError` lines in the post-restart filtered tail.
  - Every post-restart Telegram turn logged `provider=claude-cli` from the `agent/cli-backend` subsystem and emitted `claude live session reuse` / `claude live session turn` events. This confirms the request-layer CLI dispatch (`runCliAgent` → `claude` binary subprocess) is unchanged — normal turns still flow through the user's `claude` binary, not the PI fallback added by this patch. The PI fallback is only reached by `maybeCompactAgentHarnessSession`, which is the intended scope.

- **What was not tested**: `google-gemini-cli` runtime end-to-end (no Gemini CLI installation available); covered by unit test in this PR only. Full `pnpm test` matrix beyond the targeted `selection.test.ts` file — happy to run on request. The bundled-JS hot-patch on my local install is semantically equivalent to this PR's source change (uses hardcoded `claude-cli`/`google-gemini-cli` literals because importing into bundled output is impractical; the source PR uses `isCliRuntimeAlias()`).

## Tests added

- `routes CLI backend aliases to the PI harness so in-process compaction works` — verifies `selectAgentHarness` returns the PI harness for both `claude-cli` and `google-gemini-cli` runtimes.
- `lets maybeCompactAgentHarnessSession proceed for claude-cli model runs via PI` — direct regression test for the user-facing bug: the compaction entrypoint resolves without throwing `MissingAgentHarnessError` for a `claude-cli`-aliased anthropic model.

## Related

- Same-shape-of-bug PR: #72499 (helper paths bypassing CLI dispatch — compaction was missed there)
- Issue with the same error message but different root cause (auth migration gap): #84604
- Issue that pointed me at this area: #57326

## AI-assisted disclosure

This patch was authored end-to-end via Claude Code (claude-opus-4-7). The PR author (me) ran the test suite and repo check personally, validated the bundled-JS equivalent of the patch on the live install that originally exhibited the bug (log evidence above), and read every line before pushing. The original bug surfaced on my own machine — the agent's job was to localize the throw site, design the minimum-viable fix consistent with existing patterns (mirroring the codex-implicit fallback), and write the regression tests.
