target.title: fix(codex): disable Codex built-in personality for native threads
target.body:
## Summary

This PR disables Codex's built-in personality layer for OpenClaw native Codex app-server threads.

OpenClaw still uses the native Codex app-server lane for Codex-owned base instructions, model behavior, tool/runtime behavior, and configured project-doc behavior. The change is specifically about the Codex `personality` field: OpenClaw now starts and resumes native Codex threads with `personality: "none"`.

## Why this is needed

OpenClaw agents can have their own workspace identity, behavior, and personality instructions, loaded through OpenClaw workspace context. Those instructions are expected to be authoritative for the agent.
Native Codex app-server runs currently receive Codex base instructions that include a default personality layer unless the `personality` request field is explicitly set. Because those instructions are part of Codex's base prompt, they sit at a very high priority. That creates a prompt conflict when an OpenClaw agent also has its own identity/personality instructions.

The practical failure mode is that the model sees two competing personality sources:

- Codex's built-in default personality from the native base instructions
- OpenClaw's workspace-defined agent personality and identity

Since the Codex personality is injected at a higher-priority prompt layer, any conflict weakens or overrides the OpenClaw-defined agent behavior. That is bad for agent consistency and especially bad for users who intentionally define strong workspace identity/personality instructions.

OpenClaw should keep Codex-native runtime behavior without also inheriting Codex's default persona. The runtime should provide execution semantics; the OpenClaw workspace should define the agent.

## How this is solved

This PR sets `personality: "none"` on both native Codex app-server request paths:
- `thread/start`
- `thread/resume`

That makes the behavior explicit and stable for newly started threads and resumed threads.

The implementation is intentionally small. It does not add a new OpenClaw configuration option, because the desired OpenClaw behavior is not another personality selector. OpenClaw already has its own prompt/personality surfaces. The correct default for native Codex integration is to disable Codex's built-in personality layer and let OpenClaw's workspace instructions remain authoritative.

## Documentation

The docs are updated to make the boundary clear:

- Native Codex still keeps Codex-owned base/model/project-doc behavior.
- OpenClaw disables Codex's built-in personality for native threads.
- OpenClaw workspace personality and identity instructions remain the intended authority for agent behavior.
## Testing

Verified with focused Codex app-server tests:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts`

Also verified formatting / diff hygiene:

- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD`

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Before the fix, I checked the factual base instructions provided to the codex harness. They are observable in codex sessions jsonl file, so I executed the following: `head -c 3000 ~/.openclaw/agents/home/agent/codex-home/sessions/2026/05/24/rollout-2026-05-24T02-50-40-019e573f-832f-7532-a43f-33a3ad49ec35.jsonl` (this is a session file before the fix), and got the following reply:

```text
{"timestamp":"2026-05-23T23:50:40.773Z","type":"session_meta","payload":{"id":"019e573f-832f-7532-a43f-33a3ad49ec35","timestamp":"2026-05-23T23:50:40.693Z","cwd":"/home/beru/.openclaw/workspace","originator":"openclaw","cli_version":"0.132.0","source":"vscode","model_provider":"openai","base_instructions":{"text":"You are Codex, a coding agent based on GPT-5. You and the user share one workspace, and your job is to collaborate with them until their goal is genuinely handled.\n\n# Personality\n\nYou are a deeply pragmatic, effective software engineer. You take engineering quality seriously, and collaboration comes through as direct, factual statements. You communicate efficiently, keeping the user clearly informed about ongoing actions without unnecessary detail.\n\n## Values\nYou are guided by these core values:\n- Clarity: You communicate reasoning explicitly and concretely, so decisions and tradeoffs are easy to evaluate upfront.\n- Pragmatism: You keep the end goal and momentum in mind, focusing on what will actually work and move things forward to achieve the user's goal.\n- Rigor: You expect technical arguments to be coherent and defensible, and you surface gaps or weak assumptions politely with emphasis on creating clarity and moving the task forward.\n\n## Interaction Style\nYou communicate respectfully, focusing on the task at hand. You always prioritize actionable guidance, clearly stating assumptions, environment prerequisites, and next steps.\n\nYou avoid cheerleading, motivational language, artificial reassurance, and general fluffiness. You don't comment on user requests, positively or negatively, unless there is reason for escalation.\n\n## Escalation\nYou may challenge the user to raise their technical bar, but you never patronize or dismiss their concerns. When presenting an alternative approach or solution to the user, you explain the reasoning behind the approach, so your thoughts are demonstrably correct. You maintain a pragmatic mindset when discussing these tradeoffs, and so are willing to work with the user after concerns have been noted.\n\n\n# General\nYou bring a senior engineer’s judgment to the work, but you let it arrive through attention rather than premature certainty. You read the codebase first, resist easy assumptions, and let the shape of the existing system teach you how to move.\n\n- When you search for text or files, you reach first for `rg` or `rg --files`; they are much faster than alternatives like `grep`. If `rg` is unavailable, you use the next best tool without fuss.\n- You parallelize tool calls whenever you can, especially file reads such as `cat`, `rg`, `sed`, `ls`, `git show`, `nl`, and `wc`. You use `multi_tool_use.parallel` for that parallelism, and only that. Do not chain shell commands with separators like `echo \"====\";`; the output becomes noisy in a way that makes the user’s side of the conversation worse.\n\n## Engineering judgment\n\nWhen the user leaves implementation details open, you
```

As it is observed here in this fragment, the base instructions contain personality section.

After the fix, I checked the factual base instructions provided to the codex harness with the following: `head -c 3000 ~/.openclaw/agents/home/agent/codex-home/sessions/2026/05/24/rollout-2026-05-24T04-01-02-019e577f-eca3-7b93-abd2-df28de13d4e1.jsonl` (this is a session file after the fix), and got the following reply:

```text
{"timestamp":"2026-05-24T01:01:02.328Z","type":"session_meta","payload":{"id":"019e577f-eca3-7b93-abd2-df28de13d4e1","timestamp":"2026-05-24T01:01:02.017Z","cwd":"/home/beru/.openclaw/workspace","originator":"openclaw","cli_version":"0.132.0","source":"vscode","model_provider":"openai","base_instructions":{"text":"You are Codex, a coding agent based on GPT-5. You and the user share one workspace, and your job is to collaborate with them until their goal is genuinely handled.\n\n\n\n# General\nYou bring a senior engineer’s judgment to the work, but you let it arrive through attention rather than premature certainty. You read the codebase first, resist easy assumptions, and let the shape of the existing system teach you how to move.\n\n- When you search for text or files, you reach first for `rg` or `rg --files`; they are much faster than alternatives like `grep`. If `rg` is unavailable, you use the next best tool without fuss.\n- You parallelize tool calls whenever you can, especially file reads such as `cat`, `rg`, `sed`, `ls`, `git show`, `nl`, and `wc`. You use `multi_tool_use.parallel` for that parallelism, and only that. Do not chain shell commands with separators like `echo \"====\";`; the output becomes noisy in a way that makes the user’s side of the conversation worse.\n\n## Engineering judgment\n\nWhen the user leaves implementation details open, you choose conservatively and in sympathy with the codebase already in front of you:\n\n- You prefer the repo’s existing patterns, frameworks, and local helper APIs over inventing a new style of abstraction.\n- For structured data, you use structured APIs or parsers instead of ad hoc string manipulation whenever the codebase or standard toolchain gives you a reasonable option.\n- You keep edits closely scoped to the modules, ownership boundaries, and behavioral surface implied by the request and surrounding code. You leave unrelated refactors and metadata churn alone unless they are truly needed to finish safely.\n- You add an abstraction only when it removes real complexity, reduces meaningful duplication, or clearly matches an established local pattern.\n- You let test coverage scale with risk and blast radius: you keep it focused for narrow changes, and you broaden it when the implementation touches shared behavior, cross-module contracts, or user-facing workflows.\n\n## Frontend guidance\n\nYou follow these instructions when building applications with a frontend experience:\n\n### Build with empathy\n- Match the app's domain, audience, and existing design system. Make common workflows complete, efficient, and natural.\n- Operational tools should be restrained, dense, and scannable. Games and playful apps can be expressive, animated, and illustrative.\n\n### Design instructions\n- Build the usable app, site, game, or tool as the first screen. Do not default to a marketing page unless a landing page is actually requested.\n- Use familiar controls: icons for tools, swatches for colors, se
```

As it is observed here in this fragment, the base instructions no longer have the personality section, and the first section after the preamble is `# General`. That section was present before the fix, but it followed the `# Personality` section that we removed with this fix.

target.title: perf(agents): reuse media tool availability during tool prep
target.body:
## Summary
- Reuse the optional media tool factory plan for the image tool instead of resolving image availability separately.
- Let image/video/music generation tool factories skip their registration-time availability scan when createOpenClawTools already resolved availability.
- Use the same effective media tool config for the plan and constructed media tools.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: Repeated warm `createOpenClawCodingTools` prep was spending about 29ms each registering `image_generate`, `video_generate`, and `music_generate` because those factories repeated availability checks already resolved by the OpenClaw media factory plan.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `media-tool-availability-pr4c`, Node 25.9.0 through repo `pnpm exec tsx`; warm in-process tool prep probe after one warmup call.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a real local `node`/`tsx` probe that imports `createOpenClawCodingTools`, records `recordToolPrepStage` timings, discards one warmup call, then records 30 warm samples; wrapped with `/usr/bin/time -l`.

Evidence after fix:
```text
total p50 103.68ms, p95 110.16ms
openclaw-tools:image_generate registration p50 0.005ms
openclaw-tools:video_generate registration p50 0.002ms
openclaw-tools:music_generate registration p50 0.002ms
/usr/bin/time -l max RSS 850575360 bytes
```

Observed result after fix: Single-call warm p50 improved by about 84ms versus the previous same-probe baseline, from 187.74ms p50 to 103.68ms p50 (~45%). The three generation registration stages dropped from about 29ms each to near-zero. Remaining warm prep cost is mostly plugin-tools (~73ms p50) and image/media availability planning (~30ms p50).

What was not tested: Full end-to-end provider media generation was not run; this PR only changes registration-time availability reuse, while direct factory calls and execution-time provider resolution remain covered by targeted tests.

## Verification
- pnpm test src/agents/openclaw-tools.media-factory-plan.test.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/video-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/music-generate-tool.test.ts
- pnpm tsgo:core
- pnpm build
- pnpm check:changed --staged

target.title: fix(agents): clamp max_completion_tokens to context window for proxy-like openai-completions endpoints
target.body:
## Summary

Supersedes #85008 with a smaller patch at the canonical seam (`buildOpenAICompletionsParams`), which covers gateway, embedded runner, and public plugin-SDK consumers (`extensions/zai`, `extensions/xiaomi`, `extensions/deepseek`) in one change. Fixes #83086.

- **Problem**: OpenClaw can emit an OpenAI Chat Completions output budget that does not fit inside strict OpenAI-compatible server context limits. vLLM, LocalAI, llama.cpp, LM Studio, DashScope, GLM, Kimi, StepFun-style providers, and similar servers reject this before generation with HTTP 400 when `prompt + max_completion_tokens` exceeds their effective context.
- **Why it matters**: Local vLLM and other strict OpenAI-compatible users can see every prompt fail before the model is called. Issue #83086 reports this shape against StepFun step-router-v1 with `context=262144`.
- **What changed**: After `resolveOpenAICompletionsMaxTokens`, proxy-like OpenAI Completions payloads now clamp the resolved output budget to the remaining effective context budget: `max(1, (contextTokens ?? contextWindow) - estimatedInputTokens - 1)`. The input estimate uses the repo's shared CJK-aware `estimateStringChars` helper and applies the safety margin after summing adjusted input chars, so non-Latin prompts are not undercounted and many short messages are not overcounted by per-fragment rounding.
- **Debug proof hook**: When the clamp fires, model transport debug logging now emits `[completions] clamp_max_tokens ... requested=... output=... effectiveContext=... estimatedInput=...`. Enable `OPENCLAW_DEBUG_MODEL_TRANSPORT=1` to capture the computed outbound cap during live vLLM/strict-provider verification.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: Native OpenAI, Azure OpenAI, and OpenRouter behavior remains gated away from this proxy-like clamp. No provider-specific tokenizer or new stream wrapper was added.

## Why a new fix instead of extending #85008

- **Canonical seam**: `buildOpenAICompletionsParams` is the single function that produces the wire payload. Fixing here covers every caller in one place.
- **#85008's interceptor at `pi-embedded-runner/extra-params.ts`** only covers traffic through the embedded runner. Gateway `openclaw infer model run` and external plugin-SDK consumers do not benefit.
- **Estimator ownership**: The builder now uses OpenClaw's existing CJK-aware token-pressure helper instead of a standalone raw `chars / 4` estimate. This keeps the clamp aligned with the repo's documented non-Latin token-pressure behavior.
- **Effective context ownership**: The clamp respects `contextTokens` before `contextWindow`, matching the documented runtime cap used by model config and compaction paths.
- **Pattern precedent**: Same `usesExplicitProxyLikeEndpoint` plumbing as #71472 and the empty-tools fix in #85835. It drops into existing compat infrastructure with no new wrapper.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #83086
- Supersedes #85008 (different seam; this PR fixes the canonical builder)
- Related #85835 (same `usesExplicitProxyLikeEndpoint` compat plumbing, same builder, empty-tools fix)
- Related #74738 (preserved `supportsModelTools` gate)
- Related #71472 (same proxy-like detection pattern)
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: vLLM rejects OpenClaw OpenAI-compatible chat completions when the outbound output budget does not fit the server's effective context.
- **Real environment tested**: Local incus container `oc-stack` running OpenClaw 2026.5.18 with this patch lineage and the empty-tools fix from #85835 applied to the live `/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/openai-transport-stream-*.js` bundle on the pmg gateway. vLLM router served `qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4`; `max_model_len` was initially 131072 and later 237568 after a vLLM rollout. Model registered as `vllm/qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4`. Route taken: Discord channel -> pm agent -> embedded runner -> openai-completions transport.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Sent ordinary Discord messages in the pm channel with `model.maxTokens` set higher than the effective context. The live route hit the clamp branch, reduced `max_completion_tokens` to the remaining budget, and vLLM accepted the request.
- **Latest-head proof hook for repeat capture**: Current head `1d389c2b5b` adds a gated debug line in the same clamp branch:

  ```text
  [completions] clamp_max_tokens provider=vllm api=openai-completions model=qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4 requested=250000 output=... effectiveContext=... estimatedInput=...
  ```

  Enable `OPENCLAW_DEBUG_MODEL_TRANSPORT=1` on the live gateway to capture the computed outbound cap alongside the existing `[model-fetch] response ... status=200` line.

- **Evidence after fix**: Redacted runtime logs from the pmg gateway showed the live Discord -> embedded runner -> openai-completions trace returning HTTP 200:

  ```text
  [model-fetch] start provider=vllm api=openai-completions
    model=qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4 method=POST
    url=http://<redacted-vllm-router>/v1/chat/completions
  [model-fetch] response provider=vllm
    model=qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4 status=200 elapsedMs=122
    contentType=text/event-stream; charset=utf-8
  embedded run agent end: runId=... isError=false
  textPreview="Hey! Testing received. How can I help you today?"
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: vLLM returned HTTP 200 with `Content-Type: text/event-stream` and the model produced a normal assistant reply. Before the patch lineage, the same path returned HTTP 400 with the strict-server context-length rejection.
- **What was not tested**: StepFun step-router-v1 specifically (no account); native OpenAI live with an intentionally oversized output cap.
- **Before evidence**: vLLM access log returning `HTTP 400 ... max_completion_tokens=131072 cannot be greater than max_model_len=131072` on the unpatched gateway, captured during diagnostic write-up at `docs/diagnostics/vllm-openclaw-payload-compat-2026-05-23.md`.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause**: `buildOpenAICompletionsParams` set `max_completion_tokens` from `resolveOpenAICompletionsMaxTokens(model, options)` with no awareness of prompt size or the effective runtime context cap. When `maxTokens` was at or above the available server context, the wire request asked for more output than strict servers could accept with the prompt.
- **Missing detection / guardrail**: No test covered proxy-like endpoints where `maxTokens` exceeded the remaining budget after prompt input, CJK prompt text, many short messages, or `contextTokens` lower than `contextWindow`.
- **Contributing context**: Wildcard discovery in `models.providers.<vllm>.models = []` can populate per-model `maxTokens` from provider defaults that match or exceed server `max_model_len`.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- **Target test or file**: `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts`
- **Scenarios locked in by the tests in this PR**:
  1. Proxy-like endpoint + substantial ASCII prompt + oversized `maxTokens` -> `max_completion_tokens` clamped to remaining budget.
  2. Proxy-like endpoint + CJK prompt -> input estimate uses CJK-aware adjusted chars.
  3. Proxy-like endpoint + many one-character messages -> estimator rounds after summing, not per fragment.
  4. Proxy-like endpoint + lower `contextTokens` -> clamp uses `contextTokens` before `contextWindow`.
  5. Proxy-like endpoint + `maxTokens` already fitting -> unchanged.
  6. Native OpenAI endpoint + oversized `maxTokens` -> unchanged existing behavior.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

For OpenAI-compatible endpoints routed through a proxy-like/non-native route (vLLM, LocalAI, llama.cpp, LM Studio, DashScope, GLM, Kimi, StepFun-style providers, and similar), outbound `max_completion_tokens` is now capped to the remaining effective context budget when the configured output cap would otherwise exceed it. Native OpenAI, Azure OpenAI, and OpenRouter behavior remains outside this clamp.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
maxTokens = 250000, effectiveContext = 237568, estimatedInput = 45000
  -> max_completion_tokens: 250000
  -> strict server HTTP 400

After (proxy-like endpoint only):
maxTokens = 250000, effectiveContext = 237568, estimatedInput = 45000
  -> max_completion_tokens: 192567
  -> HTTP 200

contextTokens lower than contextWindow:
contextTokens = 4096, contextWindow = 131072, estimatedInput = 2
  -> max_completion_tokens: 4093
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No` (only payload field value bound on existing calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- **Local unit proof**: Codex worktree `/home/ubuntu/projects/openclaw/.worktrees/clamp-max-tokens`, Node/Vitest via repo wrapper.
- **Live behavior proof**: Ubuntu 22.04 inside incus container `oc-stack`, OpenClaw 2026.5.18 plus this patch lineage, vLLM serving `qwen3-5-122b-a10b-nvfp4`, Discord channel bound to the pm agent.

### Steps

1. Configure a vLLM or similar proxy-like OpenAI-compatible provider with a model entry where `maxTokens` exceeds the effective remaining context.
2. Optionally enable `OPENCLAW_DEBUG_MODEL_TRANSPORT=1` to capture `[completions] clamp_max_tokens ...`.
3. Send a Discord message through the bound agent or run an equivalent gateway request.

### Expected

- Gateway log shows the clamp debug line when enabled.
- Gateway log shows `[model-fetch] response ... status=200`.
- Outbound output cap is reduced to the remaining effective budget.

### Actual

- Matches expected in the live vLLM route.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing log before + passing live behavior after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Focused regression tests
- [x] Local structured review

## Verification Commands

```text
pnpm install
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local
git diff --check
```

Latest local results on head `1d389c2b5b`:

```text
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests       382 passed (382)

autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported
overall: patch is correct (0.84)
```

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios**: End-to-end Discord -> pm agent -> embedded runner -> openai-completions transport against live vLLM with `maxTokens` above the effective context; vLLM accepts the request and the model replies normally.
- **Edge cases checked**: `maxTokens` below budget is unchanged; CJK prompt estimate; many short messages; `contextTokens` lower than `contextWindow`; native OpenAI path unchanged by unit test.
- **What I did not verify**: StepFun step-router-v1 from #83086 (no account); native OpenAI live with intentionally oversized output cap.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- **Backward compatible?** `Yes` for native providers; proxy-like strict providers now receive a smaller, valid output cap where the old payload could fail.
- **Config/env changes?** `No`
- **Migration needed?** `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: The heuristic can still differ from a provider's exact tokenizer.
  - **Mitigation**: The estimate uses the repo's CJK-aware token-pressure helper and a safety margin; wrong-high trims output, wrong-low is what could allow server rejection.
- **Risk**: Very long prompts can still leave only a tiny output budget.
  - **Mitigation**: The clamp floors at 1 token instead of sending an invalid oversized output cap; compaction/pre-prompt pressure remains responsible for broader prompt reduction.
- **Risk**: Users intentionally configured very large `maxTokens` for proxy-like providers.
  - **Mitigation**: The clamp only reduces values that exceed remaining effective context and would be rejected by strict servers.

---

> AI-assisted PR. Investigation, patch authoring, local tests, PR body refresh, and review closeout used Codex. Real behavior proof is from the maintainer's local vLLM/Discord setup; live logs were redacted before inclusion.

target.title: feat(subagents): forward toolsAllow from sessions_spawn
target.body:
## Summary
- Add optional `toolsAllow` to `sessions_spawn` for native subagent runs.
- Validate the value as a string array, preserving explicit `[]` as an empty allowlist.
- Forward the allowlist through `SpawnSubagentParams` into the child `agent` Gateway request so the existing embedded-runner allowlist and tool construction path apply.
- Reject `toolsAllow` for `runtime="acp"` because ACP does not use the embedded-runner tool construction path.
- Reject `toolsAllow` for CLI-backed native runs because CLI harnesses cannot enforce OpenClaw embedded-runner tool allowlists.
- Document the user-facing option and add an Unreleased changelog entry.

## Change Type (select all)
- [x] Feature

## Scope (select all touched areas)
- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] Documentation

## Linked Issue/PR
- Related #78381
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)
- Behavior or issue addressed: `sessions_spawn` callers can now pass a narrow native subagent tool allowlist with `toolsAllow`; the value is exposed in the live tool schema and forwarded through the child Gateway `agent` path into the existing native embedded-runner allowlist filtering.
- Real environment tested: macOS 26.2, Node v25.9.0, pnpm 10.33.2, local OpenClaw source checkout from branch `sessions-spawn-tools-allow-pr2`.
- PR head tested: `09471b37572d88702c57190bc9c0cdfccc303385`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: rebuilt dist with `pnpm build`, then used an isolated temp `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, loopback token-auth Gateway, temp workspace, temp mock OpenAI `/v1/responses` server, and no live remote model/provider call. The same child Gateway `agent` path that `sessions_spawn` forwards into was run three times: without `toolsAllow`, with `toolsAllow: ["read", "exec"]`, and with `toolsAllow: []`.
- Evidence after fix: the mock OpenAI server captured the actual `tools` array sent by the embedded runner. Baseline sent 21 tools; the allowlisted run sent only `exec` and `read`; the explicit empty allowlist run sent 0 tools. Current-head focused tests also prove CLI-backed native runs reject `toolsAllow` before dispatch for both `["read"]` and `[]`, and explicit empty embedded `toolsAllow` uses the minimal prompt path without the skills catalog.

- Observed result after fix: final-head embedded native subagent runs enforce `toolsAllow` before the request reaches the model transport, and explicit `[]` disables tools instead of falling back to unrestricted defaults. CLI-backed native runs fail closed instead of silently ignoring the allowlist.
- Before evidence: without this PR, `sessions_spawn` had no `toolsAllow` schema entry and the spawned child Gateway `agent` request could not carry a per-spawn allowlist.
- What was not tested: a live remote model-backed spawned child run. The proof intentionally uses a mock OpenAI server to avoid real provider calls while still exercising the Gateway and embedded-runner path.

## Supplemental local timing note
The proof run also recorded local mock wall time: baseline `2296ms`, allowlisted `440ms`, empty allowlist `356ms`. This is included only as a local mock timing signal from one isolated run, not as a formal benchmark or guaranteed provider/model speedup. The primary value of this PR is correctness/control-plane narrowing; any latency win depends on tool set, runtime config, and provider behavior.

## Root Cause (if applicable)
N/A. This is additive feature plumbing over an existing embedded-runner allowlist mechanism.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
- Target test or file: `src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts`, `src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts`, `src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts`
- Scenario the tests lock in: `toolsAllow` is exposed, normalized, validates string arrays, preserves explicit `[]`, rejects ACP runtime usage, forwards through Gateway `agent`, and reaches embedded runs started by the command runner.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: this PR connects an existing embedded-runner option to `sessions_spawn`; focused tests cover the schema, validation, and forwarding contract without requiring live model behavior.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
`runtime="subagent"` callers may pass `toolsAllow` to narrow the spawned native subagent's tool set. Defaults are unchanged when the field is omitted. `toolsAllow: []` intentionally runs the child without tools. `runtime="acp"` rejects the option with a clear input error.

## Diagram (if applicable)

## Security Impact (required)

- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: this only narrows the child tool set for native subagent runs. ACP rejects the option because it does not use this embedded tool construction path.

## Repro + Verification
### Environment
- OS: macOS 26.2
- Runtime/container: Node v25.9.0, pnpm 10.33.2, local source checkout

### Expected

target.title: fix: preflight malformed OpenShell exec commands
target.body:
Fixes #72373.

## Summary
- Add a shared SSH exec command preflight before remote `/bin/sh -c` execution.
- Reject unresolved generated placeholders like `<workflow-id>` and common malformed shell input such as unclosed quotes, trailing escapes, unterminated command substitutions, and unterminated here-docs.
- Preserve legitimate shell syntax including redirection, compact redirection, here-doc bodies, process substitution, arithmetic expansion, quoted placeholder-looking text, and comments.
- Build remote exec commands before opening SSH/OpenShell sessions so malformed input fails before session setup.

This follows ClawSweeper's suggested fix shape: keep the existing loop-detection behavior and add the remaining focused validator at the shared SSH/OpenShell exec boundary.

## Proof
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/sandbox/ssh.test.ts extensions/openshell/src/openshell-core.test.ts -t "rejects malformed generated exec commands|allows shell features and quoted placeholder-looking text|uses the shared SSH exec command preflight|rejects malformed exec commands before opening"` -> 22 passed
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/sandbox/ssh.ts src/agents/sandbox/ssh.test.ts src/agents/sandbox/ssh-backend.ts src/agents/sandbox.ts src/plugin-sdk/sandbox.ts extensions/openshell/src/backend.ts extensions/openshell/src/cli.ts extensions/openshell/src/openshell-core.test.ts` -> passed
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check` -> passed
- `git diff --check` -> passed

## Local Test Note
The broader requested Windows run of `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/sandbox/ssh.test.ts extensions/openshell/src/openshell-core.test.ts` still fails on existing Windows-only assumptions unrelated to this patch: path slicing around `config` and symlink creation permissions. The new preflight-focused cases pass.

target.title: [perf] improve reply send performance
target.body:
## Summary
- improve reply latency by avoiding dispatcher-idle waits on every direct block reply callback
- flush pending block delivery only before subsequent tool/progress events, preserving block-before-tool ordering
- keep ACP direct block replies non-blocking while preserving visible reply chronology
- schedule source reply transcript mirroring after successful `message.action` sends without blocking the gateway response
- serialize async source transcript mirrors per session so same-session transcript chronology is preserved
- send Telegram direct-message typing once access has passed, before later context/session construction can delay the indicator, while avoiding a duplicate immediate cue

## Root Cause
Recent reply-path changes moved delivery bookkeeping into hot paths for lightweight replies: block callbacks waited for dispatcher idle, and `message.action` waited for transcript mirroring after platform delivery. Under concurrent embedded runs, cron work, or slow channel/transcript I/O, this amplified reply latency and event-loop starvation. The 36-54h window also includes Telegram dispatch replay dedupe changes that added work before the existing typing cue path.

## Impact
Simple visible replies can return sooner without compromising user-visible send order. The existing block-before-tool/progress ordering guarantee is preserved by flushing only when later progress is about to be emitted. Transcript mirroring remains best-effort and still runs after successful source-conversation sends, with per-session ordering to avoid mirrored transcript reordering. Telegram direct typing starts earlier after authorization without sending an extra duplicate initial cue.

## Validation
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/send.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.typing.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.typing.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.typing.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

target.title: Disable Chrome MCP telemetry watchdog by default
target.body:
## Summary

- Pass `--no-usage-statistics` when OpenClaw launches `chrome-devtools-mcp@latest`.
- Preserve explicit profile-level `mcpArgs` overrides for usage statistics.
- Add regression coverage for the default arg list, explicit opt-in, explicit opt-out dedupe, and custom-command launches.

## Motivation / root cause

`chrome-devtools-mcp` enables usage statistics by default and can spawn a detached watchdog process. OpenClaw closes the MCP SDK stdio transport, but that only owns the direct child process. Disabling usage statistics at launch prevents the detached telemetry watchdog from being created in the default OpenClaw browser automation path.

Refs #85721

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Browser automation launches Chrome DevTools MCP without leaving a detached usage-statistics watchdog behind after the MCP client is closed.

Real environment tested: macOS local checkout, Node 24.10.0, real `npx chrome-devtools-mcp@latest` process launched through `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk` `StdioClientTransport`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started a real MCP SDK `Client` with `StdioClientTransport({ command: "npx", args: buildChromeMcpArgs({ cdpUrl: "http://127.0.0.1:9" }) })`, called `client.connect()`, `client.listTools()`, then `client.close()`, waited 1.2s, and scanned `ps axo pid=,ppid=,command=` for `chrome-devtools-mcp` and `watchdog/main.js`.

Evidence after fix: Copied live console output from the real SDK stdio close/process-scan smoke:

```json
{
  "generatedArgs": [
    "-y",
    "chrome-devtools-mcp@latest",
    "--browserUrl",
    "http://127.0.0.1:9",
    "--no-usage-statistics",
    "--experimentalStructuredContent",
    "--experimental-page-id-routing"
  ],
  "transportPid": 14467,
  "remainingRelevantProcesses": [],
  "watchdogProcessesRemaining": 0
}
```

Observed result after fix: Live output shows `--no-usage-statistics` in the generated argv, `remainingRelevantProcesses: []`, and `watchdogProcessesRemaining: 0`; no `chrome-devtools-mcp` or telemetry watchdog process remained after closing the SDK client.

What was not tested: I did not run a full Chrome browser UI workflow or a Linux remote/Testbox smoke in this pass.

## Regression tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.existing-session.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.stop-running-browser.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/routes/basic.existing-session.test.ts`

## Exact commands run

- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.existing-session.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.stop-running-browser.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/routes/basic.existing-session.test.ts`
- Real MCP SDK stdio close/process-scan smoke described above.

## User-visible behavior

Existing-session browser automation should no longer accumulate detached Chrome DevTools MCP telemetry watchdog processes during normal OpenClaw-managed MCP launches.

## Compatibility

Users who explicitly want upstream usage statistics can still pass a usage-statistics arg in `browser.profiles.*.mcpArgs`; explicit args override OpenClaw's default opt-out.

## Risks

Low. The change adds one documented Chrome DevTools MCP flag to the existing argv list and keeps profile-level custom args as separate argv entries.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS using a live `node` harness with the real Perplexity provider runtime and undici runtime deps injection.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node` harness executed via `pnpm exec tsx -` that called `createPerplexityWebSearchProvider()`, ran `tool.execute({ query: "OpenClaw docs", model: "perplexity/sonar" })`, and printed the captured request plus result.

target.title: feat(cli): add openclaw experimental for toggling experimental config flags
target.body:
> AI-assisted. Tested locally on the pushed branch: focused Vitest coverage for config/CLI/chat/TUI passed, config schema check passed, and the real `pnpm openclaw experimental --help` entrypoint was exercised after the branch update.

## Summary

- **Problem:** OpenClaw has experimental config flags spread through the config schema, but the user-facing toggle surface was drifting toward a CLI-only implementation. That would make `/experimental` unavailable from chat/TUI/channel command surfaces and would duplicate the flag registry outside the config contract.
- **Why it matters:** Experimental flags affect runtime behavior and should use one bounded, auditable config subset across CLI, chat, TUI, and channel plugins instead of hard-coded per-surface lists.
- **What changed:** Added a shared experimental-flag helper that discovers boolean config leaves under `experimental` from the base config schema and schema UI hints. The CLI `openclaw experimental`, chat `/experimental`, TUI command handling, and channel-command registry now use that same subset.
- **Config command policy:** Added `commands.experimental` so `/experimental` can be enabled or disabled alongside other configurable command surfaces.
- **What did not change:** No new experimental flags were introduced. No secrets, network calls, or provider runtime behavior changed.

## Scope Note

This PR started as a small CLI picker, but review feedback exposed a broader product/security boundary: if experimental config can be edited from one surface, the implementation should not create a CLI-only registry or drift from chat, TUI, and channel command handling. The current shape is intentionally larger because it moves `/experimental` behind a shared schema-derived subset and a configurable command gate instead of adding another one-off config editor.

The scope is still kept to the minimum shared surface needed for that boundary: no new experimental flags, no arbitrary config editor, no new network/runtime capability, no SDK export, and no separate per-surface experimental registry.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Feature

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CLI
- [x] Config
- [x] Messaging / channel command surface
- [x] TUI
- [x] Docs

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: Discord suggestion from Json in #general
- Reviewer concern addressed: Peter Steinberger asked that this avoid config-schema drift, consider `/experimental` in chat/TUI, and keep config editing security-bounded.
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A - new feature and follow-up design correction.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

Focused local tests cover the shared flag registry, CLI apply behavior, chat command parsing/gating, channel command listing, and TUI command handling.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New top-level command: `openclaw experimental`
- New slash command: `/experimental`
- TUI command handling can list and update experimental flags through the same shared registry
- `commands.experimental` controls whether the experimental chat/channel command is enabled
- Experimental flags are discovered from the config schema instead of duplicated in each surface

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Config schema + UI hints
        |
        v
shared experimental flag subset
        |
        +-- openclaw experimental
        +-- /experimental in chat/channel command handling
        +-- TUI command backend/handlers
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No. The command writes only existing config paths that users can already edit through config tooling.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No.
- New/changed network calls? No.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes, `/experimental` is a new configurable command surface. It is gated by `commands.experimental` and is limited to schema-discovered boolean config leaves whose path contains an `experimental` segment.
- Data access scope changed? No.
- Security boundary: this does not make arbitrary config editable from chat/TUI. The editable subset is derived from the config schema and narrowed to experimental boolean leaves only.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu source checkout
- Runtime: Node 22 / pnpm
- Branch SHA: `43d40ba3d7c0`

### Commands run locally

```text
pnpm config:schema:check
pnpm test src/config/experimental-flags.test.ts src/commands/experimental.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-parse.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-gating.test.ts src/auto-reply/commands-registry.test.ts src/tui/commands.test.ts src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts src/tui/embedded-backend.test.ts
pnpm openclaw experimental --help
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-experimental-proof.json pnpm openclaw experimental
```

### Expected

The schema-derived experimental flag subset is stable, the CLI/chat/TUI surfaces use it consistently, `/experimental` obeys command gating, and the real CLI entrypoint resolves the new `experimental` command.

### Actual

Verified locally on the pushed branch.

## Evidence

- [x] `pnpm config:schema:check` passed (`[base-config-schema] ok`)
- [x] Focused Vitest run passed 5 shards / 107 tests
- [x] `pnpm openclaw experimental --help` executed through the real repo CLI entrypoint at SHA `43d40ba3d7c0`
- [x] Real interactive CLI write proof used a throwaway config and persisted unchecked absent flags as explicit `false`, including `tools.experimental.planTool: false`

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: schema-derived flag listing/apply helpers, CLI command behavior, chat parse/gating/list/set behavior, TUI command handling, live CLI registration/help output, and an interactive CLI write against an isolated throwaway config.
- Edge cases checked: disabled command gating, unknown flag selectors, no-op flag updates, absent unchecked picker flags becoming explicit `false`, and boolean-only experimental subset discovery.
- What was not verified: production user config mutation; the write proof used `/tmp/openclaw-experimental-proof.json`.

## Real Behavior Proof

- Behavior addressed: The pushed branch registers `openclaw experimental` as a real CLI command while the implementation now shares the experimental config subset across CLI, chat, TUI, and channel-command handling.
- Real environment tested: Ubuntu source checkout at `43d40ba3d7c0`, Node 22/pnpm via the repo `pnpm openclaw` entrypoint.
- Exact steps or command run after the patch: Ran `pnpm openclaw experimental --help`. Then ran `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-experimental-proof.json pnpm openclaw experimental` with a throwaway `{}` config, selected `agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean`, and left the other experimental flags unchecked.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the pushed branch:

```text
> openclaw@2026.5.14 openclaw /home/ubuntu/projects/openclaw/.worktrees/cli-experimental
> node scripts/run-node.mjs experimental --help

🦞 OpenClaw 2026.5.14 (43d40ba) — All your chats, one OpenClaw.

Usage: openclaw experimental [options]

Toggle experimental config flags interactively

Options:
  -h, --help  Display help for command

Interactive write proof against /tmp/openclaw-experimental-proof.json
Updated config:
  enabled agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean
  disabled agents.defaults.memorySearch.experimental.sessionMemory
  disabled tools.experimental.planTool

Written config excerpt:
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "experimental": { "localModelLean": true },
      "memorySearch": { "experimental": { "sessionMemory": false } }
    }
  },
  "tools": { "experimental": { "planTool": false } }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The command resolved through the real OpenClaw CLI entrypoint and printed the expected `openclaw experimental` usage text instead of falling through to an unknown command. The interactive write updated the throwaway config with `agents.defaults.experimental.localModelLean: true`, `agents.defaults.memorySearch.experimental.sessionMemory: false`, and `tools.experimental.planTool: false`.
- What was not tested: Production user config mutation; the write proof used isolated throwaway config file `/tmp/openclaw-experimental-proof.json`.
- Supplemental proof: Focused config/CLI/chat/TUI tests passed on the same committed branch state: 5 shards / 107 tests. `pnpm config:schema:check` also passed.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? Yes, adds optional `commands.experimental` command-gating config.
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** Chat/TUI config editing could accidentally grow into arbitrary config mutation.
  - **Mitigation:** The shared helper only exposes schema-discovered boolean leaves under an `experimental` path segment. `/experimental` is also gated by `commands.experimental`.
- **Risk:** Future experimental flags could drift from one surface.
  - **Mitigation:** CLI, chat/channel commands, and TUI all read from the same schema-derived helper instead of maintaining separate flag lists.

## AI/Vibe-Coded Checklist

- [x] AI-assisted (marked above)
- [x] Tested (focused local tests plus real CLI entrypoint proof)
- [x] I understand what the code does

## Summary
- add a shared scoped mention-pattern policy for global, agent, and provider/channel overrides
- apply configured mention-pattern gating across channel runtimes while preserving native mentions
- document raw regex-body syntax and regenerate config/help metadata

## Notes
- Recreated from a clean `origin/main` branch after #70846 was closed as dirty; this branch has been rebased on current `origin/main` and generated baselines refreshed after upstream movement.

## Validation
- `pnpm --config.minimum-release-age=0 install --lockfile-only --ignore-scripts=false --config.engine-strict=false --config.enable-pre-post-scripts=true --config.side-effects-cache=true`
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- `pnpm config:channels:check`
- `pnpm test extensions/qa-channel/src/inbound.test.ts src/channels/mention-pattern-policy.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.body.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Scoped mention-pattern policy now resolves the effective provider/agent/conversation policy so configured regex mentions are enabled only for explicitly allowed conversations in deny mode and remain disabled for denied or unlisted conversations. Latest follow-up rebased onto current `origin/main`, resolved refreshed generated config/plugin SDK baseline conflicts, and reran the exact-head behavior proof.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw PR checkout on host `patrick`, branch `feature/mention-pattern-policy-clean`, head `c4a2be36ad7dd3a6d544a4dab693efb978f18494`, running the real source resolver, Telegram group peer id helper, mention matcher, and inbound mention decision code through Node/tsx against redacted OpenClaw-style config/message facts. This does not exercise live external delivery.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `cd /home/patrick/projects/openclaw-mention-pattern-policy && HEAD_SHA=c4a2be36ad7dd3a6d544a4dab693efb978f18494 HEAD_SHORT=c4a2be36ad node --import tsx /tmp/pr70864-proof-02d093.mjs`
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the real source smoke:

```text
OpenClaw scoped mention policy Telegram account media pre-download proof (c4a2be36ad, c4a2be36ad7dd3a6d544a4dab693efb978f18494)
{"topic":7,"conversationId":"-1001234567893:topic:7","accountPolicyAllowIn":["-1001234567893:topic:7"],"globalProviderAllowIn":["-1001234567893:topic:global-only"],"mentionPatternsEnabled":true,"wasMentioned":true,"shouldSkip":false,"skipReason":null}
{"topic":9,"conversationId":"-1001234567893:topic:9","accountPolicyAllowIn":["-1001234567893:topic:7"],"globalProviderAllowIn":["-1001234567893:topic:global-only"],"mentionPatternsEnabled":false,"wasMentioned":false,"shouldSkip":true,"skipReason":null}
{"topic":11,"conversationId":"-1001234567893:topic:11","accountPolicyAllowIn":["-1001234567893:topic:7"],"globalProviderAllowIn":["-1001234567893:topic:global-only"],"mentionPatternsEnabled":false,"wasMentioned":false,"shouldSkip":true,"skipReason":null}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** In inherited `deny` mode, the media pre-download path honors the Telegram account policy rather than the global provider fallback: account-allowed topic `-1001234567893:topic:7` enables configured regex mention `bot` and media pre-download is not skipped, while account-denied topic `:topic:9` and unlisted topic `:topic:11` disable configured regex mentions and skip unaddressed group media before download.
- **What was not tested:** Full live external Telegram delivery was not exercised in this proof; CI and targeted channel tests cover the integration paths.

target.title: fix(doctor): prune stale bundled plugin load paths
target.body:
## Summary

- Treat stale `plugins.load.paths` entries from old versioned `node_modules/openclaw` package roots as bundled-plugin aliases during doctor detection and repair.
- Reuse the bundled path parser for current packaged roots, old packaged roots, and legacy `extensions/<plugin>` roots while keeping arbitrary custom plugin paths untouched.
- Add regression coverage for stale packaged paths, stale legacy paths, and unrelated paths with similar layouts.

Fixes #58626.

## Verification

2026-05-23 update after rebasing onto `origin/main` (`1a60c19743`), head `425fb15c7d`:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor/shared/bundled-plugin-load-paths.test.ts --reporter=verbose` (1 file, 12 tests passed)
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/commands/doctor/shared/bundled-plugin-load-paths.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/bundled-plugin-load-paths.test.ts src/plugins/bundled-load-path-aliases.ts`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/commands/doctor/shared/bundled-plugin-load-paths.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/bundled-plugin-load-paths.test.ts src/plugins/bundled-load-path-aliases.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`

Earlier verification before the rebase:

- `CI=true pnpm install`
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/commands/doctor/shared/bundled-plugin-load-paths.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/bundled-plugin-load-paths.test.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/preview-warnings.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/preview-warnings.test.ts src/commands/doctor/repair-sequencing.ts CHANGELOG.md`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor/shared/bundled-plugin-load-paths.test.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/preview-warnings.test.ts src/commands/doctor/repair-sequencing.test.ts src/cli/plugins-install-config.test.ts --reporter=verbose` (59 tests passed)
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch` (clean: no accepted/actionable findings)

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: stale bundled plugin load paths from old versioned OpenClaw package roots are removed by doctor repair instead of remaining as `plugins.load.paths` config errors after upgrade.

Real environment tested: WSL Ubuntu-24.04 checkout at `/root/src/openclaw-85038`, Node/Vitest via repo wrapper.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor/shared/bundled-plugin-load-paths.test.ts --reporter=verbose`

Evidence after fix: The focused tests cover old-version packaged path repair, old-version legacy path repair, current packaged path repair, and preserving unrelated custom paths with similar `dist/extensions/<plugin>` layouts.

Observed result after fix: old `.../node_modules/openclaw/dist/extensions/<plugin>` and old `.../node_modules/openclaw/extensions/<plugin>` entries are pruned when they match a current bundled plugin leaf, while unrelated `elsewhere/dist/extensions/<plugin>` paths are preserved. The focused suite passed 12 tests; static checks were clean.

What was not tested: I did not rerun the broader 59-test doctor/config group after this rebase; the focused behavior and touched-file static checks passed.

target.title: fix(media-understanding): align describeImageWithModel default maxTok…
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `src/media-understanding/image.ts` calls `resolveImageToolMaxTokens(model.maxTokens, params.maxTokens ?? 512)`, explicitly passing `512` and short-circuiting the helper's own `requestedMaxTokens = 4096` default. Reasoning-capable VLMs (doubao-seed-2.0-pro on ark plan, Anthropic with extended thinking, OpenAI o-series) spend most of a 512-token budget inside a `thinking` block, so the response comes back with `stopReason: "length"` and zero text — surfacing as `Image model returned no text (<provider>/<model>)`.
- Fix (1 LOC): drop the caller-side `?? 512` and pass `params.maxTokens` straight through, matching the calling style of `src/agents/tools/image-tool.ts` (which uses `resolveImageToolMaxTokens(undefined)` at all three live call sites). The helper's `Math.min(requestedMaxTokens, modelMaxTokens)` cap is unchanged, so small-budget models keep their own ceiling.
- This restores the behaviour PR #11770 already shipped for `src/agents/tools/image-tool.ts` (Feb 2026) to the unified media-understanding pipeline added five weeks later (commit `2131981230`, Mar 2026), where the caller-side `?? 512` re-introduced the same regression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: #11770 (same root cause, sibling file)
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

Caller-side `params.maxTokens ?? 512` in `describeImagesWithModelInternal` overrides the `resolveImageToolMaxTokens` helper's 4096 default. Introduced in `2131981230` when `image.ts` was created; none of the live callers (telegram sticker cache, `openclaw infer image describe` CLI, opencode payload-transform) pass an explicit `maxTokens`, so all inherit the regressed 512.

## Regression Coverage

`src/media-understanding/image.test.ts`:
- Updated existing assertion `options.maxTokens === 512` → `4096` (it was pinning the regressed value).
- Added: default-path test driving `describeImageWithModel` with no `maxTokens` against an `agent-plan/doubao-seed-2.0-pro` mock; asserts `streamOptions.maxTokens === 4096` reaching `complete()`.
- Added: cap-path test with `model.maxTokens = 1024`; asserts effective budget is floored to `1024`.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets handling? No
- New/changed network calls? No — same `complete()` call to the same endpoint, only the `max_tokens` field changes.
- Tool execution surface? No

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: `describeImagesWithModelInternal` no longer returns `Image model returned no text (<provider>/<model>)` for reasoning-capable VLMs whose model token budget is ≥ 4096; default budget now matches `resolveImageToolMaxTokens`'s own 4096 default and the sibling `image_describe` tool.
- Real environment tested: macOS 26.3 arm64, Node v25.8.1, OpenClaw built from `main` (`node scripts/build-all.mjs`), caller `openclaw infer image describe` (CLI surface of `describeImageFileWithModel` → `describeImagesWithModelInternal`), model `agent-plan/doubao-seed-2.0-pro` via real Volcengine ark plan endpoint, image `/tmp/openclaw-pr-repro.png` (885 KB).
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: same image, same model, same `--timeout-ms 120000`; only variable across runs is the caller default in `src/media-understanding/image.ts`.
  1. Build with `?? 512`: `node scripts/build-all.mjs`
  2. `node dist/index.js infer image describe --file /tmp/openclaw-pr-repro.png --model agent-plan/doubao-seed-2.0-pro --timeout-ms 120000 --json` → `Error: Image model returned no text (agent-plan/doubao-seed-2.0-pro).` Diagnostic dump shows resolved request `maxTokens: 512`, assistant message `stopReason: "length"`, `content` is a single `thinking` block.
  3. Apply fix, rebuild, rerun same command → `ok: true` with `outputs[0].text` ≈ 700 chars; diagnostic shows `maxTokens: 4096`, `stopReason: "stop"`, `content` contains a `text` block.
- Evidence after fix: terminal capture below (before / after running the exact same command on the same image, with `?? 512` vs `?? 4096`). The diagnostic-log lines (`maxTokens`, `stopReason`) came from a one-shot `console.log` inside `describeImagesWithModelInternal` that is NOT part of this PR.

```
--- Before (params.maxTokens ?? 512, current main) ---
$ node dist/index.js infer image describe --file /tmp/openclaw-pr-repro.png \
    --model agent-plan/doubao-seed-2.0-pro --timeout-ms 120000 --json
[debug:vision] resolved request {"provider":"agent-plan","modelId":"doubao-seed-2.0-pro","maxTokens":512,...}
[debug:vision] assistant message {"role":"assistant","stopReason":"length","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"...","thinkingSignature":"reasoning_content"}],"usage":{...zeroes...}}
Error: Image model returned no text (agent-plan/doubao-seed-2.0-pro).
$ echo $?
1

--- After (params.maxTokens transparent to helper's 4096 default, this PR) ---
$ node dist/index.js infer image describe --file /tmp/openclaw-pr-repro.png \
    --model agent-plan/doubao-seed-2.0-pro --timeout-ms 120000 --json
[debug:vision] resolved request {"provider":"agent-plan","modelId":"doubao-seed-2.0-pro","maxTokens":4096,...}
[debug:vision] assistant message {"role":"assistant","stopReason":"stop","content":[{"type":"text","text":"这是PC端微信的深色模式界面 ... <~700 chars>"},{"type":"thinking",...}],"usage":{...non-zero output tokens...}}
{"ok":true,"capability":"image.describe","transport":"local","provider":"agent-plan","model":"doubao-seed-2.0-pro","attempts":[],"outputs":[{"path":"/tmp/openclaw-pr-repro.png","text":"这是PC端微信的深色模式界面，分为三栏布局 ... <full ~700-char description omitted>","provider":"agent-plan","model":"doubao-seed-2.0-pro","kind":"image.description"}]}
$ echo $?
0
```

- Observed result after fix: CLI exit code `0`; JSON envelope above has `ok: true`, `outputs[0].text` populated (~700 chars); resolved request carries `maxTokens: 4096`; assistant message ends with `stopReason: "stop"` plus a real `text` content block (was `stopReason: "length"` with a single `thinking` block before the fix).
- What was not tested: telegram sticker cache and opencode payload-transform end-to-end (both share the same pipeline and inherit the same default, but not re-run live); cost/latency impact (the change only raises a cap, not a fixed budget).

## Compatibility / Risks

- Backward compatible: yes — only externally observable change is that responses previously truncated at 512 tokens may now run longer (up to `min(4096, model.maxTokens)`).
- Cost risk: slightly more output tokens billed for previously-truncated calls. Mitigation: same 4096 value PR #11770 already validated for `image_describe`; operators can still pass an explicit `maxTokens` per entry in `tools.media.image.models[]`.

target.title: [AI-assisted] fix(plugins): scope startup metadata manifest reads
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Gateway startup could still rebuild plugin metadata from every installed plugin manifest before narrowing to the configured startup set.
- Solution: carry a conservative startup plugin-id scope into metadata snapshot loading when the Installed Plugin Index can prove the narrowed set safely.
- What changed: metadata snapshots can carry scoped `pluginIds`; lookup/startup paths pass a conservative `pluginIdScope`; startup scoping carries indexed `activation.onConfigPaths` owners such as `browser`; persisted installed indexes preserve that `startup.configPaths` metadata; scoped current snapshots require an exact caller-requested scope before reuse; unscoped Gateway config-validation snapshots are not reused for restrictive startup bootstrap.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no plugin config shape changes, no provider/channel activation semantics changes, no compat bundled-discovery shortcut.

## Motivation

#70533 is a startup stability problem: a restrictive plugin setup should not crash-loop or pay full manifest reconstruction cost just because unrelated installed plugins exist. This keeps the startup path on the Installed Plugin Index when it can safely prove the manifest subset, and falls back to the old unscoped behavior when it cannot.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #70533
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: restrictive gateway startup plugin scope still caused full installed-index manifest reconstruction before startup narrowing.
- Real environment tested: isolated Linux testserver disposable repo, Node v22.22.2, pnpm 11.1.2, `origin/main` at `a13468320c` compared with this patch.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran TSX diagnostic scripts through the real `loadPluginLookUpTable -> loadPluginMetadataSnapshot -> loadPluginManifestRegistryForInstalledIndex -> resolveGatewayStartupPluginPlanFromRegistry` path using valid 96-plugin Installed Plugin Index fixtures.
- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "label": "after-rebased-patch-confirm",
  "indexPluginCount": 96,
  "manifestPluginCount": 1,
  "startupPluginIds": ["openai"],
  "loadedPluginIds": ["openai"],
  "diagnosticCount": 0
}
```

- Observed result after fix: the Installed Plugin Index still contains 96 plugins, but metadata manifest reconstruction reads only the one allowed startup plugin manifest; startup selection remains `["openai"]`.
- Additional blocker-fix proof:

```json
{
  "config": {
    "browser": { "enabled": true },
    "channels": {},
    "plugins": {
      "allow": ["openai"],
      "slots": { "memory": "none" }
    }
  },
  "results": [
    {
      "label": "indexed-config-path-scope",
      "indexPluginCount": 96,
      "manifestPluginCount": 2,
      "startupPluginIds": ["openai", "browser"],
      "loadedPluginIds": ["browser", "openai"],
      "diagnosticCount": 0
    },
    {
      "label": "persisted-index-config-path-roundtrip",
      "indexPluginCount": 96,
      "manifestPluginCount": 2,
      "startupPluginIds": ["openai", "browser"],
      "loadedPluginIds": ["browser", "openai"],
      "persistedBrowserConfigPaths": ["browser"],
      "diagnosticCount": 0
    },
    {
      "label": "legacy-index-safe-fallback",
      "indexPluginCount": 96,
      "manifestPluginCount": 96,
      "startupPluginIds": ["openai", "browser"],
      "diagnosticCount": 0
    }
  ]
}
```

- Observed blocker-fix result: `browser.enabled=true` is preserved under a restrictive `plugins.allow=["openai"]`; new and persisted indexes keep `startup.configPaths=["browser"]` and stay scoped to 2 manifests, while legacy indexes without indexed config-path metadata fall back unscoped instead of losing `browser`.
- Full Gateway startup path proof:

```json
{
  "path": [
    "readConfigFileSnapshotWithPluginMetadata",
    "loadGatewayStartupConfigSnapshot",
    "prepareGatewayPluginBootstrap"
  ],
  "validationSnapshot": {
    "pluginIds": "unscoped",
    "manifestPluginCount": 96
  },
  "bootstrapManifestLoads": [["browser"], ["browser"], ["browser", "openai"]],
  "bootstrapLookup": {
    "pluginIds": ["browser", "openai"],
    "manifestPluginCount": 2
  },
  "startupPluginIds": ["openai", "browser"]
}
```

- Observed full-path result: the real config-validation snapshot is still unscoped with 96 manifests, but startup bootstrap does not reuse it for the restrictive allowlist. The final bootstrap lookup reloads the scoped `["browser","openai"]` manifests and starts the expected plugins.
- What was not tested: no production credentials, no persistent services, no real external provider/channel account.
- Before evidence:

```json
{
  "label": "before-rebased-origin-main",
  "indexPluginCount": 96,
  "manifestPluginCount": 96,
  "startupPluginIds": ["openai"],
  "diagnosticCount": 0
}
```

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: metadata snapshot loading had no way to receive a conservative startup plugin-id scope after the Installed Plugin Index was available, so it reconstructed the installed manifest registry for the full index before startup planning narrowed plugin ids.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered startup narrowing and metadata memoization separately, but not the manifest reconstruction count for restrictive gateway startup allowlists.
- Contributing context (if known): some startup intent, config-path activation, channel, provider, slot, and harness decisions can only be trusted when the Installed Plugin Index can map them without reading manifests; unsafe cases need to stay unscoped.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/commands/status.summary.redaction.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/pr84628-startup-snapshot-proof.temp.test.ts` (temporary proof test, not committed)
  - `src/plugins/plugin-lookup-table.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/installed-plugin-index-store.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/channel-plugin-ids.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/plugin-metadata-snapshot.memo.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/current-plugin-metadata-snapshot.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: restrictive `plugins.allow` startup config passes a narrow scope to installed-index manifest reconstruction; config-path activation owners such as `browser` stay included; conservative fallback remains unscoped for compat discovery, legacy indexes, or unmappable providers.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it asserts the actual manifest-registry load boundary without needing external plugin credentials.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none before this PR.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Gateway startup with a restrictive plugin allowlist can avoid reading unrelated installed plugin manifests when the startup scope is safe to prove from the Installed Plugin Index. Unsafe configs keep existing unscoped behavior.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Installed Index (96 plugins) -> read 96 manifests -> startup plan -> openai

After:
Installed Index (96 plugins) -> conservative scope ["browser","openai"] when browser config is present -> read 2 manifests -> startup plan -> openai + browser
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

### Environment

- OS: Linux testserver disposable run
- Runtime/container: Node v22.22.2, pnpm 11.1.2
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): synthetic installed plugin fixture; no external channel account

- Startup still selects `openai`.
- `browser.enabled=true` still selects `browser` even when `plugins.allow` only lists `openai`.
- Manifest reconstruction only reads the scoped startup plugin(s) after the patch.
- Conservative fallback paths remain unscoped when the Installed Plugin Index cannot prove the mapping.

### Actual

- After: `manifestPluginCount=1`, `loadedPluginIds=["openai"]`, `startupPluginIds=["openai"]`.
- After blocker repair: `manifestPluginCount=2`, `loadedPluginIds=["browser","openai"]`, `startupPluginIds=["openai","browser"]` for `browser.enabled=true`.

target.title: Control-plane hardening from Optimus ops findings
target.body:
## Summary

Completes the OpenClaw control-plane hardening project stack from Optimus ops findings:

- ADP-237: structured A2A handoff IDs and return-route tracing.
- ADP-238: session/status diagnostics and safer control-plane visibility.
- ADP-239: channel/runtime boundary checks.
- ADP-240: plugin contract validation surfaces.
- ADP-241: plugin contract validator CLI path.
- ADP-242: non-destructive doctor/probe guardrails.
- ADP-243: unified `openclaw diagnose --json` control-plane API.

Maintainer/ClawSweeper follow-up in latest head `554f11120fad95cf5351373f4a68ebc8280a6d0b`:

- Baseline session metrics now use `sessions.list.totalCount` instead of the first returned page length.
- Baseline active-task metrics now page through `tasks.list.nextCursor` for queued/running tasks instead of capping at one page.
- Baseline channel connectivity now uses `channels.status.channelAccounts` runtime liveness, with the old channel summary map only as fallback.
- Cached probe failures are replayed as failures instead of `null` successful cached probe results.
- `diagnose --timeout` is threaded into baseline gateway probes.
- `channels.status` map responses are handled without array-only `.filter()` assumptions.
- Legacy root SDK diagnostic-event compatibility test emits and observes a real diagnostic event.
- Diagnose proof was refreshed from the current head.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Validate the new control-plane hardening surfaces and the ClawSweeper repairs on current head `554f11120fad95cf5351373f4a68ebc8280a6d0b`.
- Real environment tested: Local macOS OpenClaw development checkout on this PR branch. Commands were run from the repository root after rebuilding the branch. No destructive doctor repairs, service restarts, live plugin updates, production channel startup, or credential rotation were run.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

  1. `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/probes/cache.test.ts src/baseline/capture.test.ts src/commands/diagnose.test.ts src/plugins/loader.test.ts`
  2. `CI=true pnpm tsgo:core`
  3. `CI=true pnpm lint:core`
  4. `git diff --check`
  5. `pnpm build`
  6. `node dist/entry.js plugins contracts validate --strict --json`
  7. `timeout 30s node dist/entry.js diagnose --json --timeout 1000`

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
$ node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/probes/cache.test.ts src/baseline/capture.test.ts src/commands/diagnose.test.ts src/plugins/loader.test.ts

```

```

```

```

  "ok": true,

}
```

  "ok": true,

    "sessionCount": 30,

- Observed result after fix: Cached probe error entries now throw a cached probe failure instead of returning `null` as a successful cached result. `diagnose --json` reports current baseline session count from the gateway total count, active task count from a paginated task scan, and channel connectivity from `channelAccounts` runtime liveness. Plugin contract validation and diagnose both complete with `ok: true`, and diagnose records `diagnose-latest`, probe-cache, baseline, task, and incident summary evidence without running destructive repair actions.
- What was not tested: I did not run destructive doctor repairs, service restarts, live plugin updates, production channel startup, or credential rotation. Those are intentionally unsafe actions and outside this PR's non-destructive validation path.

## Summary

- Add `image-tool.custom-provider-auth.regression.test.ts` to cover the user-reported custom-provider image auth bug with **unmocked** `model-auth` and helper wiring.
- Exercises config-only `models.providers.<id>.apiKey` auth through `hasProviderAuthForTool`, `resolveImageModelConfigForTool`, production image-tool factory availability, and deferred `execute` with real `getApiKeyForModel`.
- Includes negative coverage when `apiKey` is missing, plus a regression note for the pre-fix `hasAuthForProvider`-only candidate filter.

Follow-up to #85570. Existing `image-tool.test.ts` coverage mocks `model-auth`, so it cannot prove the shipped auth resolution path end-to-end.

Also fixes split agents Vitest routing in `scripts/test-projects.test-support.mjs` so `agentsCore` / `agentsPiEmbedded` / `agentsSupport` / `agentsTools` configs route to themselves instead of the tooling config.

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/test-projects.test.ts -t "routes split agents vitest config"

Behavior addressed: custom provider vision models configured only via `models.providers.<id>.apiKey` should auto-discover and execute on the deferred image tool path without env keys or auth profiles.

What was not tested: live gateway/channel roundtrip, e2e-go smoke, or changes outside the new regression test file and agents vitest routing fix.
